attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Original creator. A systematic retelling of Monteiro Lobato's entire body of work through the eyes, heart, and spiritual life of Tia Nastácia. She was never the cook — she was always the author. This grammar composts Lobato by giving Nastácia the voice his books denied her: her feelings about the family, her mission toward the children, her Yoruba ancestry, her secret prayers, her conversations with the spirits of the land.

attribution.name: Monteiro Lobato
attribution.note: Creator of the Sítio do Picapau Amarelo universe. Works entered Brazilian public domain 2019. This grammar does not reproduce Lobato's text — it retells his entire library from the perspective of the woman he wrote as background. The original stories are compost; what grows here is hers.

name: Nastácia Conta — She Was Always the Author
description:
A retelling of Monteiro Lobato's entire Sítio do Picapau Amarelo universe through the eyes of Tia Nastácia — the Black woman Lobato wrote as a cook, but who was always the keeper of the stories, the herbs, the prayers, and the children. Each item is a book or episode from Lobato's library, told the way Nastácia lived it: what she was thinking in the kitchen while adventures happened in the yard, what she prayed for at night, which spirits she spoke to in the forest, what she really felt about the family she served and the children she loved more than their parents understood.

Nastácia carries Yoruba ancestry. She goes to church on Sunday and bate a cabeça — but not because she reveres the Christian idols above. Her gods are buried beneath the church floor. She placed them there herself. Every time she bows, she reciprocates with the animist forces her ancestors were faithful to. She found her way to keep faith even in the middle of colonial erasure.

Written simply, so older children can read it and begin to learn about the Yoruba spiritual world that survived slavery, syncretism, and a century of children's books that made the cook invisible.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura) from J. Borges and other northeastern Brazilian artists. Percy Lau's 'Tipos e Aspectos do Brasil' illustrations of Brazilian folk life (1940s IBGE series). Pierre Verger's photographs of Candomblé and Umbanda ceremonies (selected public domain images, 1940s-50s). Tarsila do Amaral's modernist paintings of rural Brazilian life (works entering public domain). Jean-Baptiste Debret's illustrations of Black life in colonial Brazil (1834-1839, public domain).

items.name: The Day Narizinho Went to the Stream

sections.Nastácia Tells:
They say the story begins with Narizinho going to the stream. That's how Dona Benta tells it, and that's how the books have it. A little girl with a turned-up nose, walking to the water with a doll in her arms.

But I'll tell you how it really began.

It began with me standing at the stove at five in the morning, stirring mingau with one hand and praying with the other — not out loud, because Dona Benta doesn't like when I pray out loud, she says it's superstition. So I pray with my hand. I stir clockwise and I ask Oxum to watch the water. I ask her because Narizinho goes to that stream every day and nobody in this house thinks to ask the river if that's alright.

Oxum is the orixá of fresh water, of rivers and streams and waterfalls. She is a mother. She protects children near water. She likes honey and mirrors and the color gold. In the old country — in Bahia, where my mother's people came from the ships — the women would bring honey to the riverbank and sing for Oxum before letting any child wade in.

Here at the Sítio, I can't bring honey to the stream. Dona Benta would ask questions. So I stir the mingau clockwise and I pour a little honey in it, and when Narizinho eats her breakfast before going to the stream, Oxum's sweetness is already in her belly. That's how you protect a child when you can't say the prayer out loud. You hide it in the food.
sections.What the Book Says: In Lobato's Reinações de Narizinho (1931), the story opens with Narizinho walking to the Ribeirão (stream) on Dona Benta's farm, where she falls asleep and enters the Kingdom of Waters — an underwater world of talking fish. It is a famous beginning, full of wonder. Nastácia appears in the background, cooking.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Every river has a spirit. The Yoruba people call her Oxum — Ọ̀ṣun — and she crossed the ocean in the bodies of enslaved women who refused to forget. When they arrived in Brazil, they found rivers that looked nothing like the Ọ̀ṣun River in Nigeria, but the spirit was there anyway, because water is water and a mother is a mother.

When Narizinho enters the Kingdom of Waters in Lobato's story, she's doing something that Yoruba children have always known how to do: she's visiting the ones who live below the surface. Lobato thought he invented this. He didn't. My grandmother's grandmother knew.
sections.The Kitchen Prayer: Oxum, mãe das águas doces — watch this child today. She doesn't know your name but she goes to your house every morning. Keep the water gentle. Keep the fish kind. Bring her back to me before the beans are done.
sections.For Young Readers: In Yoruba tradition, rivers are not just water — they are home to a spirit called Oxum (say: oh-SHOOM). Oxum loves children and protects anyone who comes to her waters with respect. When enslaved Africans were brought to Brazil, they couldn't practice their religion openly. So they found quiet ways to keep it alive — a prayer hidden in the stirring of a pot, a little honey slipped into breakfast, a song hummed so softly only the river could hear it.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Washerwomen at the Laranjeiras River — Black women at the water

items.name: The Day the Doll Got a Voice

sections.Nastácia Tells:
I sewed Emília from scraps. Cloth from an old dress, button eyes, yarn hair. People say she's a rag doll. That's what the books call her — boneca de pano.

But here is what nobody asks: what was I thinking while I sewed her?

I was thinking about my mother. My mother could sew anything — she sewed dolls for the children of the house where she worked, same as me. But when she sewed, she sang. And the songs were not lullabies. They were pontos — sacred songs from the terreiro, from the gira, from the circle where the spirits come down. She told me once: 'When you make something with your hands and sing the right words, the thing you make carries the song inside it. It might not sing out loud. But it remembers.'

So when I sewed Emília, I sang a ponto for Erê — the child spirit, the playful one, the sacred troublemaker who says what adults are afraid to say. I didn't think it would actually work.

Then one morning Narizinho came running into the kitchen: 'Tia Nastácia! Emília is talking!'

And I thought: Mãe, you were right.
sections.What the Book Says: In Lobato's story, Emília gains the power of speech after swallowing Doctor Caramujo's magic pills in the Kingdom of Waters. From that moment she never stops talking — she is rude, brilliant, bossy, and completely ungovernable. Lobato presents this as fantasy and comedy. Nobody asks where the doll came from or what the woman who made her put into her.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
In Yoruba tradition and its Brazilian continuations (Candomblé, Umbanda), an erê is a child spirit — playful, honest, sometimes inconvenient. Erês come during ceremonies and speak truths that adults have learned to hide. They laugh when things are serious. They ask questions that break the room open.

Emília behaves exactly like an erê. She says what nobody else dares to say. She mocks Visconde's European knowledge. She refuses to obey. She disrupts every hierarchy she encounters.

Lobato thought he created a funny character. What he actually did was channel something that was already in the cloth — because the woman who sewed the doll knew what she was doing, even if the man who wrote the book didn't.
sections.The Ponto:
Erê, erê, come and play
Erê, erê, what will you say?
The grown-ups forgot how to speak
So the child-spirit speaks for the weak

(This is a simplified version. Real pontos are sung in Yoruba-Portuguese and belong to specific terreiros. Nastácia would have known the real ones from her mother.)
sections.For Young Readers:
An erê (say: eh-REH) is a child spirit in Afro-Brazilian spiritual traditions. When erês arrive during a ceremony, they play, they laugh, they say things that surprise everyone. They remind grown-ups that being serious all the time means you've forgotten something important.

When Nastácia sewed Emília, she wasn't just making a toy. She was sewing with hands that carried generations of knowledge about how to put spirit into matter. Her mother taught her. Her mother's mother taught her mother. The knowledge traveled across an ocean in the bodies of people who were told their traditions were nothing — but the traditions survived anyway, in the stitching, in the singing, in the things that came alive when no one was looking.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: A woman of ordinary fortune in her domestic interior — sewing and household work

items.name: When Pedrinho Caught the Saci

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Pedrinho came home one night with a bottle. Inside the bottle: a Saci.

He was so proud. 'Look, Tia Nastácia! I caught him! Uncle Barnabé told me how — you wait at the crossroads with a sieve and when the whirlwind comes, you throw the sieve and trap the Saci inside, then stuff him in a bottle with a cross on the cork.'

I looked at that bottle and my blood went cold.

The Saci is not a toy. The Saci is not a character in a children's story. The Saci is a force — older than the Portuguese, older than the fazendas, as old as the forest. The Tupi-Guarani people knew him as the protector of the woods, the one-legged guardian who punishes those who take from the forest without asking.

My friend Raizeira — an old cabocla woman who sold herbs at the market in town — she told me once: 'The Saci is what the forest sends when people forget to ask permission. He tangles your hair so you have to slow down. He hides your things so you have to pay attention. He whistles at night so you remember you're not alone.'

And this boy — this white boy from São Paulo who comes to the farm for holidays — he put that force in a bottle. For fun.

I didn't say anything to Pedrinho. What would I say? Dona Benta would tell me I'm being superstitious again. But that night, when everyone was asleep, I went to the yard and I set the bottle on its side with the cork loosened. Not all the way out — just enough that if the Saci wanted to leave, he could.

In the morning, the bottle was empty.

Pedrinho was furious. I told him: 'The Saci is a trickster, menino. He tricked you.'

But the truth is: I tricked him. Because some things are not meant to be caught.
sections.What the Book Says: O Saci (1921) is one of Lobato's most famous books. Pedrinho, with help from Uncle Barnabé (a Black elder who knows the forest), captures a Saci using a traditional method involving a sieve at a crossroads. The Saci becomes Pedrinho's companion and shows him the nighttime world of the forest. Lobato treats the Saci as a mischievous sprite — entertaining but contained.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The Saci comes from Tupi-Guarani tradition, where forest spirits are not entertainment — they are relations. The one-legged figure, the red cap, the pipe — these are not random details. The red cap (carapuça) holds the Saci's power; without it, he is diminished. When Pedrinho captures the Saci by taking his cap, it's a colonial gesture dressed as adventure: take the sacred object, contain the spirit, make it serve your curiosity.

Nastácia learned about the Saci from two sources: her own Yoruba-descended tradition (where Exu, the trickster orixá of the crossroads, shares the Saci's love of mischief and boundary-crossing) and from cabocla women like Raizeira — women of mixed indigenous and African descent who carried both knowledge systems and wove them together at the market, at the river, at the edge of the forest where the two worlds met.
sections.The Kitchen Prayer: Exu, dono da encruzilhada — forgive this boy. He doesn't know what he caught. Open the bottle gently. Let the forest have what belongs to the forest.
sections.For Young Readers:
The Saci (say: sah-SEE) is a one-legged spirit from Brazilian indigenous tradition. He wears a red cap, smokes a pipe, and lives in the forest. He is a trickster — he tangles horses' manes, hides household objects, and whistles in the dark. But he is not mean. He is the forest's way of reminding people: you are a guest here.

In Lobato's story, Pedrinho catches a Saci in a bottle, like catching a firefly. But Nastácia knows that some things should not be caught. The indigenous people who first told Saci stories didn't try to trap him — they listened to him. There's a difference between catching something and meeting it.

illustrations.scene: Virgin forest near Mangaratiba, Rio de Janeiro province

items.name: The Forest at Night

sections.Nastácia Tells:
They don't know that I go to the forest at night.

After the kitchen is clean and the children are asleep and Dona Benta is reading by her lamp — I put on my shawl and I walk to the edge of the trees. Not far. Just to where the yard ends and the mata begins.

Raizeira taught me this. She said: 'You don't have to go deep. The forest comes to the edge to meet you, if you're quiet enough.'

I bring an offering. Sometimes farinha. Sometimes a candle. Sometimes just my breath and my attention. I sit on the stump by the jabuticabeira and I listen.

The forest here is different from what my mother described in Bahia — the sounds are different, the trees are different. But Raizeira helped me understand: the spirits of the land speak in the language of the land. My mother's orixás traveled in her body from Africa to Bahia. They learned the rivers and the trees of the Recôncavo. When I came to the Sítio — following work, following need — I had to learn a new set of names.

The Curupira. The Caipora. The Mãe-d'água. The Boitatá. These are not my ancestors' names. These are the names the land already had before anyone arrived — names the Tupi, the Guarani, the Kaingang carried.

But here is what Raizeira showed me, and what I believe: the orixás recognize other spirits. Oxóssi, lord of the forest and the hunt, does not compete with the Curupira. They nod to each other. They are both saying the same thing: the forest is alive, and you are a guest.

So I sit at the edge of the mata and I speak to whoever is listening. Sometimes in Portuguese. Sometimes in the few words of Yoruba my mother left me. Sometimes just in silence.

And sometimes — not every night, but sometimes — the forest speaks back. A branch cracks when there is no wind. A firefly comes so close I can feel the heat of it. An owl calls three times and then stops, as if it asked a question and received an answer.

I am not a mãe-de-santo. I am not a pajé. I am a cook who prays. But prayer is what keeps the line open between the visible and the invisible, and someone in this house has to do it because Dona Benta reads books about science and the children think adventure is something that happens in the yard. Somebody has to remember that the forest is a person.
sections.What the Book Says: In O Saci, Lobato describes the forest at night as a stage for Pedrinho's adventure — full of creatures like the Cuca (an alligator-witch), the Curupira (a forest guardian with backward feet), the Saci, and others. Pedrinho meets these beings with wonder and courage. Nastácia, in the books, is afraid of the forest and the supernatural. She is the superstitious one, the fearful one.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Lobato wrote me as afraid. He was wrong — but he was also almost right. I am not afraid of the forest. I am in awe of it. And awe looks like fear to people who have never felt it.

When Dona Benta says I'm superstitious, what she means is: I believe in things she doesn't. When the books say I'm afraid of the Saci, what they mean is: I respect the Saci in a way that the characters who capture him do not.

Fear and reverence are not the same thing, but colonialism cannot tell the difference. To the colonial mind, the only correct relationship to the forest is mastery — study it, classify it, catch its creatures in bottles. Anything else is superstition.

I hold the older relationship: reciprocity. The forest gives. You give back. The spirits speak. You listen. The land has rules. You follow them. This is not fear. This is manners.
sections.For Young Readers:
Many Brazilian indigenous peoples — Tupi, Guarani, Kaingang, and hundreds of others — have names for the spirits of the forest. The Curupira has backward feet so you can't track him. The Caipora rides a wild pig and protects the animals. The Boitatá is a fire-serpent that guards the fields.

These are not fairy tales. They are ways of saying: the forest is alive and it has its own rules. When enslaved Africans arrived in Brazil carrying their own spiritual traditions, something remarkable happened — the Yoruba orixás and the indigenous forest spirits recognized each other. Oxóssi, the Yoruba spirit of forests and hunting, and the Curupira, the indigenous protector of the woods, are not the same being. But they agree on the most important thing: the forest is a person, not a resource. You don't own it. You relate to it.

illustrations.scene: Giant tree in the Brazilian tropical jungle

items.name: The Hunt — What Nastácia Heard from the Kitchen

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Caçadas de Pedrinho. The Hunts of Little Pedro. That's what they called the book.

I remember the day it happened. The children decided to hunt a jaguar — an onça. They went into the forest with Emília's big plans and Pedrinho's little rifle and all the confidence of people who believe the world was made for them to walk through.

I was in the kitchen. The beans were on. The rice was halfway done. And I was afraid — but not in the way the books say. Not the silly afraid, the rolling-eyes afraid, the 'ai meu Deus' afraid that makes people laugh.

I was afraid because the forest has teeth. And these children don't know it because nobody taught them to be guests.

When Pedrinho hunts, he hunts like a colonizer hunts: for sport, for proof, for the story he'll tell afterward. He doesn't ask the forest. He doesn't offer anything. He takes.

And the book celebrates this. It's an adventure! The brave boy in the jungle!

But there is another story in Caçadas de Pedrinho, and it's the one nobody wanted to tell. The book has words in it — words about me, about my skin, about my body — that make me a joke. 'Macaca de carvão,' one passage says. Coal monkey. 'Carne preta.' Black meat.

I was the hunted, too. In the same book where animals are prey, I am prey. Different kind of hunt, same logic: some beings are for catching, for laughing at, for putting in bottles and books.

I stirred the rice and I said nothing. What is there to say? The books are written. The words are in them. But a book is not the last word. A book is compost. You put it in the ground. You let it rot. And what grows from it is yours to name.
sections.What the Book Says: Caçadas de Pedrinho (1933) is one of the most contested books in Brazilian literary history. In 2010, the Conselho Nacional de Educação identified over 20 racist passages. The STJ case remains unresolved. The book describes Pedrinho and his friends hunting a jaguar in the forest. It also contains persistent anti-Black language directed at Nastácia — language that mirrors the dehumanization of Black people in Brazilian society.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Hunting is not evil. Indigenous peoples hunt with prayer and reciprocity — taking only what is needed, offering thanks. The hunt becomes colonial when it becomes sport: when the point is not food but dominance, not survival but proof that you can take whatever you want.

Pedrinho's hunt is a colonial hunt. The book knows this, in a way — the animals fight back, the forest resists. But the book doesn't follow that thread to its conclusion, because the book is also hunting. It's hunting me. The same pen that writes Pedrinho's adventure writes Nastácia's degradation.

This is why the book is compost. Not because it should disappear. Because it should decompose until the two hunts become visible: the one in the forest and the one on the page.
sections.For Young Readers:
This book, Caçadas de Pedrinho, is famous in Brazil — and it's also the center of an important debate. The story has exciting adventures, but it also has words in it that are hurtful to Black people. Words that call a person an animal. Words that treat dark skin as a joke.

Those words are not okay. They were not okay when they were written, and they are not okay now. But instead of pretending the book doesn't exist, we can do something harder and more honest: we can read it and notice what it does. We can see that the same story that celebrates hunting animals also hunts a Black woman with its language. And we can decide: that's not the story we want to keep telling. We compost it. We grow something new.

illustrations.scene: Jaguar hunt in the Brazilian wilderness

items.name: When Emília Wrote Her Memoirs

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Emília decided to write her memoirs. A rag doll, writing her life story. The whole house was in an uproar.

She dictated to Visconde — the corn-cob man, who thinks he knows everything because he read an encyclopedia. She bossed him around. She told him what to write and what to leave out. She lied. She exaggerated. She invented things that never happened and denied things that did.

And you know what? I respected her for it.

Because who gets to write their memoirs? People with power. People with leisure. People who believe their version of events matters. Emília — a doll made from scraps, brought to life by accident (or so they think) — she grabbed that power with both cloth hands and refused to let go.

But here's what made me laugh and cry at the same time: Emília wrote her memoirs. Nobody ever asked me to write mine.

I have worked in this house since I was a girl. I have cooked every meal, cleaned every room, held every child when they cried. I know the history of this family better than any of them. But nobody handed me a pen. Nobody said: 'Nastácia, tell your story.'

You want to know the difference between a doll and a cook? The doll is charming when she speaks out of turn. The cook is uppity.

So this — all of this, everything I'm telling you now — these are my memoirs. Not dictated to Visconde. Not approved by Dona Benta. Mine.

And unlike Emília, I won't lie. Or if I do, at least my lies will be the kind that carry a deeper truth — the way Yoruba stories do, the way Anansi lies to tell the truth, the way the erê plays to teach.
sections.What the Book Says: Memórias da Emília (1936) is one of Lobato's most celebrated books. Emília dictates her memoirs to Visconde de Sabugosa, and the result is a comic masterpiece — unreliable, egomaniacal, and brilliant. It's considered a landmark of Brazilian modernism. Nastácia appears only to cook and to be the butt of occasional jokes.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The question of who gets to write memoirs is the question of who gets to be a person. In Lobato's world, a rag doll writes her memoirs before the Black woman does. That is not an accident. That is the colonial hierarchy made visible: a thing I created with my hands has more narrative authority than I do.

But the erê energy I sewed into Emília — the sacred child-spirit — that energy doesn't respect hierarchy. The erê speaks for everyone who was silenced. When Emília talks, she is unconsciously channeling the voice I wasn't allowed to use. She is my erê. She is my protest. She just doesn't know it.
sections.For Young Readers:
Here's a question worth sitting with: In Lobato's world, a rag doll writes a whole book about her life. But the woman who sewed the doll — who has lived longer, seen more, and knows more — never gets asked to tell her story.

Why? What kind of world decides that a doll is more interesting than the woman who made her?

Nastácia's memoirs are the ones Lobato never wrote. But they exist anyway — in the food she cooked, in the prayers she whispered, in the way she held the children when they came back from adventures they thought were theirs alone.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: A Brazilian lady in her home surrounded by enslaved domestic workers

items.name: When Pedrinho Asked for Stories

sections.Nastácia Tells:
The night Pedrinho came to the kitchen and asked me to tell stories — that was the night everything changed. And also the night nothing changed.

He said: 'Tia Nastácia, you're the people. You must know the people's stories. Tell us.'

Tia Nastácia é o povo. He said it like a compliment. Maybe it was. But listen to what it means: I am not a person. I am a people. I am not Nastácia with her own thoughts and dreams and a mother she misses every single day. I am 'the people' — a category, a source, a well you lower a bucket into.

And still — still — I told the stories. Because the stories wanted to be told. They had been waiting inside me like seeds that need rain, and that boy with his bright eyes was the rain, even if he didn't know it.

I told them the Bicho Manjaléu, and the Princess Thief, and the Green Sergeant, and the Turtle and the Jaguar. I told them stories my mother told me, and stories my mother's mother brought from Bahia, and stories the caboclas shared at the market — Indigenous stories, African stories, stories that had no single owner because they belonged to the air.

And after each story, Emília would criticize. 'Too rough,' she'd say. 'Not literary enough. Dona Benta tells better stories.' And Pedrinho would compare: 'The European version is more elegant.'

And I would sit there and smile. Because what can you do? The child asks for your treasure and then tells you it's not shiny enough. That's how it works in this house. That's how it works in this country.

But the stories don't care about criticism. The stories survived slavery. They'll survive a little boy's literary opinions.
sections.What the Book Says: Histórias de Tia Nastácia (1937) is the book where Lobato comes closest to honoring Nastácia — and also the book where the hierarchy is most exposed. Pedrinho asks Nastácia to tell folk stories, declaring 'Tia Nastácia é o povo' (Tia Nastácia IS the people). She tells dozens of stories from Brazilian oral tradition. After each one, the children critique them for being less sophisticated than European tales. Lobato seems to both value and condescend to folk tradition simultaneously.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The stories I told in that kitchen are older than Lobato. Older than Brazil. The Bicho Manjaléu came from West Africa in the hulls of slave ships, carried in the only luggage enslaved people were allowed: memory. The Turtle and the Jaguar is Tupi in origin — a story about how cleverness survives where strength would be crushed.

When Emília says my stories are 'rough,' she's repeating what every colonial culture says about oral tradition: that it's primitive, unpolished, pre-literary. But oral stories are not failed books. They are a different technology — one that doesn't need paper or printing presses or copyright. They travel in breath. They adapt to every mouth that tells them. They are alive in a way that books are not.

The irony: Lobato wrote a book about oral stories and thought the book was the important part. The stories don't need the book. The book needs the stories.
sections.For Young Readers:
When Pedrinho asks Nastácia for stories, he's doing something important — he's recognizing that she carries knowledge. But he also does something hurtful: he compares her stories to European ones and decides hers aren't as good.

This happens a lot in the world. Someone asks you to share something precious, and then they grade it. They measure your grandmother's recipe against a restaurant. Your family's songs against the radio.

Nastácia's stories — the ones from Africa and from Indigenous Brazil — traveled thousands of miles without ever being written down. They were carried in human memory, which is the oldest and most powerful library in the world. A story that survived the Middle Passage doesn't need a little boy's approval. It needs an open ear.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1839
illustrations.scene: A Brazilian family in Rio de Janeiro

items.name: The Stories Underneath the Stories

sections.Nastácia Tells:
I told them the stories they could hear. But every story I told had another story underneath it — the one I kept for myself.

When I told the Turtle and the Jaguar, the children heard a funny animal fable. But underneath: the turtle is the enslaved person. Slow, small, trapped in a hard shell. The jaguar is the master — fast, beautiful, powerful. And the turtle wins. Every time. Not by fighting. By thinking. By waiting. By being smarter than the thing that wants to eat her.

My mother told me: 'The animal stories are never about animals. They're about us. The spider, the turtle, the rabbit — those are the ones who survive without claws. That's us, minha filha. We survive by wit because they took everything else.'

When I told the story of the Princess Thief, the children heard a fairy tale. But underneath: a story about a woman who takes back what was stolen from her, and the world calls her a thief for it.

When I told the Bicho Manjaléu, the children heard a monster story. But underneath: a warning about white men who come to your house pretending to be something they're not.

I could never say this out loud. In Dona Benta's house, folk stories are entertainment. They are not allowed to be political. They are not allowed to be survival.

But that's what they are. Every story a Black woman tells in a white woman's kitchen is an act of survival disguised as service.
sections.What the Book Says: In Histórias de Tia Nastácia, Lobato presents the folk tales as quaint, charming, sometimes crude alternatives to the European literary tradition. The children enjoy some and dismiss others. The book never explores the political or survival dimensions of oral tradition.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
In West African and Afro-Brazilian storytelling, the animal trickster tradition (Anansi the spider, the tortoise, the rabbit) is not children's entertainment. It is an encoded survival manual. Enslaved people could not openly discuss resistance, escape, or the psychology of surviving under domination. So they told stories about small animals outsmarting large predators — and everyone in the audience knew exactly what they meant.

This double-layered quality — a surface story for the masters, a deeper story for the community — is one of the great achievements of diasporic African culture. It survived because it was invisible to the people it was hiding from.

When Lobato put Nastácia's stories in a book, he captured the surface layer. The layer underneath — the one that matters — he never saw. Not because he was stupid, but because the stories were not designed for him to see.
sections.For Young Readers:
In many cultures, stories have two layers — like a house with a secret room. The first layer is what anyone can hear: a funny story about a turtle who tricks a jaguar. The second layer is what the storyteller's community understands: a story about how you survive when someone bigger and stronger wants to control you.

Enslaved African people in Brazil (and all over the Americas) became masters of the double story. They couldn't speak openly about their lives, so they spoke through animals and monsters and princesses. The people who enslaved them heard children's stories. The people who told them heard something else entirely.

Learning to hear the second layer is one of the most important things a reader can do.

illustrations.scene: Batuque — African drum circle and dance gathering

items.name: Why Nastácia's Portuguese Is the Real Grammar

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Emília went to the Land of Grammar. She visited the nouns and the verbs and the adjectives, all living in their proper houses, all following their proper rules. Dona Benta was so pleased. 'Learning grammar is learning to think correctly,' she said.

Correctly.

Let me tell you about my Portuguese.

I say 'nóis vai' instead of 'nós vamos.' I say 'pra mim fazer' instead of 'para eu fazer.' I say 'tá' instead of 'está' and 'né' instead of 'não é.' Every word I speak, Dona Benta's grammar would call wrong.

But my Portuguese is not wrong. My Portuguese is the Portuguese that survived. When enslaved Africans arrived in Brazil speaking Yoruba, Fon, Kimbundu, they were forced to learn Portuguese from other enslaved people — not from grammar books, not from schools. They learned it in the fields and the kitchens and the senzalas. They bent Portuguese to fit their mouths, their rhythms, their ways of thinking.

'Nóis vai' is not ignorance. It is the grammar of Kimbundu laid over the grammar of Portuguese. In Kimbundu, you don't change the verb to match the person — the verb stays the same and the context tells you who's doing what. 'Nóis vai' follows a rule. It's just not a European rule.

Every time someone corrects my Portuguese, they are saying: the way you survived is not good enough. The language you built from nothing, in chains, with no schools and no books — it's not real.

Emília went to the Land of Grammar and came back knowing the rules. Good for her. I live in the land of grammar every time I open my mouth and speak the language my people made from pieces of empire and pieces of Africa and pieces of this red soil.
sections.What the Book Says: Emília no País da Gramática (1934) is an educational adventure where Emília and friends visit a place where grammatical concepts are personified — Nouns live in one district, Verbs in another, and so on. Lobato uses this to teach standard Portuguese grammar to children. The book assumes that correct grammar equals correct thinking.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Brazilian Portuguese has always been multiple languages wearing one name. The 'standard' (norma culta) is the Portuguese of the colonizers, the elites, the academies. But the Portuguese spoken by most Brazilians — the Portuguese of the favelas, the countryside, the kitchens — carries the grammar of Bantu and Yoruba and Tupi-Guarani inside it.

Linguists now recognize what enslaved people always knew: 'nonstandard' Portuguese is not broken standard Portuguese. It is a different grammar, with its own internal logic, shaped by the meeting of African and indigenous and European language systems. The word 'caçula' (youngest child) is Kimbundu. 'Moleque' (kid) is Kimbundu. 'Acarajé' is Yoruba. 'Mandioca' is Tupi.

Nastácia's Portuguese is not a failure to learn Dona Benta's Portuguese. It is a success — the success of creating a living language from the wreckage of multiple worlds.
sections.For Young Readers:
When you speak, you're not just using words — you're using a grammar, which is the set of invisible rules that tells you how to put words together. Most people think there's one 'correct' grammar and everything else is a mistake.

But here's the truth: every community has its own grammar. And the Portuguese that Nastácia speaks — with its rhythms and shortcuts and special sounds — is not 'bad Portuguese.' It's a grammar that was created by enslaved African people who had to learn a new language under the worst conditions imaginable. They mixed Portuguese with their own languages (Yoruba, Kimbundu, Fon) and created something new. Words like 'samba,' 'batuque,' 'dendê,' and 'acarajé' are all African words that became Brazilian Portuguese.

Next time someone says a way of speaking is 'wrong,' ask yourself: wrong by whose rules?

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Baiana women in traditional dress

items.name: When Dona Benta Teaches Science

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Every evening, the serões. Dona Benta sits in her chair with a book and teaches the children about the world. The solar system. The history of inventions. The evolution of species. Physics. Chemistry.

She is very intelligent, Dona Benta. I won't pretend otherwise. She reads more than anyone I know. She explains things clearly. The children love it.

But here is what happens during the serões: I am in the kitchen. I can hear everything through the wall. And sometimes Dona Benta explains something that I know differently.

She explains the stars as burning gases millions of kilometers away. I know this is true. But I also know that the stars are the eyes of Olorum — the supreme god in Yoruba tradition, the one who sees everything. Both things are true. The stars are gas and they are eyes. The universe is not a single story.

She explains rain as water vapor condensing in clouds. I know this too. But rain is also Xangô — the orixá of thunder and justice — releasing his fury or his blessing. When it thunders before rain, that's Xangô's axé. When the rain is gentle, that's Oxum crying for her children.

She explains herbs as chemical compounds with medicinal properties. I know the chemistry. But I also know the axé — the spiritual force that lives in the leaf and can only be activated by the right prayer, the right song, the right hand.

Dona Benta teaches from above — from the sky, from the books, from Europe. I know from below — from the soil, from the body, from Africa and from this land.

Neither of us is wrong. But only one of us is invited to speak.

Sometimes, after the serões, when the children come to the kitchen for a late snack, I tell them a little extra. 'Your grandmother told you about thunder,' I say. 'But did she tell you that thunder has a name?'

They lean in. They always lean in.
sections.What the Book Says: Serões de Dona Benta (1937) and História das Invenções (1935) are Lobato's science education books. Dona Benta gives evening lectures to the children about physics, astronomy, chemistry, and the history of human ingenuity. The books are praised for making science accessible to children. Nastácia appears in the kitchen, occasionally expressing superstitious amazement.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The division between 'science' and 'superstition' is itself a colonial product. When Dona Benta explains the world through European science, she is transmitting one knowledge system — a powerful one, but one system among many. Yoruba cosmology, Indigenous Brazilian ecological knowledge, and Afro-Brazilian herbal medicine are not pre-scientific. They are differently scientific — they organize knowledge through different axioms.

Yoruba healers knew the medicinal properties of thousands of plants long before European pharmacology arrived. Tupi-Guarani farmers developed sophisticated polyculture systems that European monoculture is still trying to understand. The 'serões' of the Sítio teach the children that knowledge comes from books and Europe. The kitchen teaches something else: knowledge also comes from the body, the land, the ancestors, and the practice of listening to what is not in any book.
sections.For Young Readers:
There are many ways to know something. Science is one way — it uses experiments and measurements and math to understand the world. But it's not the only way.

Yoruba tradition says that thunder is Xangô, a powerful spirit of justice. Does that mean thunder isn't electricity in the atmosphere? No — it's both. The Yoruba name doesn't replace the scientific explanation. It adds another layer: not just what thunder IS, but what thunder MEANS.

Many indigenous peoples around the world have detailed knowledge of plants, animals, weather, and ecosystems that scientists are only now beginning to appreciate. This knowledge wasn't written in books — it was carried in stories, songs, and daily practice. It's a different library, stored in different shelves. But it's real knowledge, and it's been keeping people alive for thousands of years.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Return of a plantation owner to his home

items.name: The Trip to the Sky — What Nastácia Did While They Were Gone

sections.Nastácia Tells:
They went to the sky. The children, Emília, Visconde — they sprinkled the magic powder, Pó de Pirlimpimpim, and flew to the stars. Dona Benta watched from the porch. I watched from the kitchen window.

I did not go. I never go.

In the books, I stay behind because I'm afraid. Because I'm too heavy. Because I'm the one who needs to keep the house running while the real people have adventures.

But here is the truth: I stayed because someone has to hold the ground.

In Umbanda, we say that when spirits fly — when the medium's body receives an entity, when the soul travels to other planes — there must be someone on the earth holding the line. The cambono. The one who stays awake while others travel. The one who keeps the candles lit, the water fresh, the fire going. Without the cambono, the travelers have no anchor. They can get lost between worlds.

So while the children flew to the stars, I was the cambono. I kept the fire. I swept the kitchen. I sang quietly to myself the songs my mother taught me — songs that are not lullabies but navigation charts, songs that say: come back, come back, the earth remembers you.

And they did come back. They always come back. Not because of the magic powder. Because the kitchen was still warm when they landed.
sections.What the Book Says: Viagem ao Céu (1932) describes the children's trip to outer space using Pó de Pirlimpimpim (magic traveling powder). They visit the Moon, the stars, and meet astronomical wonders. Lobato uses the adventure to teach astronomy. Nastácia stays behind — a running pattern in the Sítio books where she is excluded from adventures.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
In many African and Afro-Brazilian spiritual traditions, the ability to 'travel' — to send your spirit to other realms — is real and sacred. Candomblé priestesses enter trance states where they visit the Orum (the spiritual world). Umbanda mediums receive spirits who travel vast distances to speak through them.

But every spiritual system that practices travel also has a role for the one who stays: the anchor, the keeper, the one who maintains the connection to earth. In Yoruba tradition, this is connected to Omolu/Obaluaê — the orixá of the earth, of disease and healing, of what is buried and what holds everything up. Someone has to be Omolu. Someone has to be the ground.

Nastácia is not excluded from the adventure. She IS the adventure's foundation. Without her, the Sítio is just a house. With her, it's a terreiro — a sacred ground from which journeys begin and to which journeys return.
sections.For Young Readers:
In many spiritual traditions, when someone goes on a journey — even a spiritual one — someone else stays behind to keep things safe. This person is not less important than the traveler. They are the anchor.

Think of it like this: an astronaut goes to space, but thousands of people on the ground keep the mission running. The person at mission control matters as much as the person in the rocket.

Nastácia stays behind in every adventure. But she's not being left out — she's holding the ground. She's the reason there's a warm kitchen to come home to. She's the reason the Sítio is still a home and not just a house.

illustrations.scene: Habitation of enslaved people — domestic life, cooking, children

items.name: European Stories at the Table

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Dona Benta read Don Quixote to the children. And Peter Pan. And the Greek myths — Hercules and his labors, Theseus and the Minotaur. She read the Iliad and the Odyssey. She read European stories like a woman opening the windows of a grand house to let in the only light she recognizes.

I was in the kitchen, always in the kitchen, and I listened.

Don Quixote fights windmills. My people fought chains. I wonder which one Dona Benta thinks is more heroic.

Peter Pan flies to Neverland. My mother's stories flew across an ocean in the bellies of ships. I wonder which journey Dona Benta thinks is more magical.

Hercules killed the Nemean Lion. The Tupi-Guarani warriors hunted the jaguar with nothing but courage and a prayer to the forest. I wonder which one gets a book.

I'm not saying European stories are bad. Don Quixote is beautiful — a man who refuses to accept the world as it is. I understand that man. I live in a house where I am the cook and the fool and the superstitious one, and every night I talk to spirits and tend a faith that's older than Portugal. If that's not tilting at windmills, I don't know what is.

But when Dona Benta reads only European stories, she is teaching the children that the world began in Greece and continued through Europe and arrived in Brazil as a gift. She doesn't mean to teach this. But the bookshelf teaches it anyway.

The bookshelf is a curriculum. And the curriculum is a worldview. And the worldview says: some stories are literature and some stories are folklore, and the difference is who has a printing press.
sections.What the Book Says: Lobato wrote several adaptations of European classics for children: Dom Quixote das Crianças (1936), Peter Pan (1930), Os Doze Trabalhos de Hércules (1944), O Minotauro (1939). These are among his most beloved books, bringing world literature to Brazilian children through Dona Benta's evening readings.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The relationship between 'literature' and 'folklore' is a colonial hierarchy. Literature is written, authored, European, canonical. Folklore is oral, collective, 'ethnic,' supplementary. But this hierarchy is artificial — the Iliad was oral before it was written. The Greek myths were folk stories before scholars decided they were 'classical.'

When Lobato retells European myths for Brazilian children, he is doing translation. When Nastácia tells African and Indigenous stories in the kitchen, she is doing the same thing. The difference is not in the act but in the value assigned to it. Dona Benta's translations become books. Nastácia's become background noise.

In Yoruba tradition, the Odù Ifá — the vast corpus of stories used in divination — is one of the richest literary systems on earth. It contains over 4,000 narratives, covering philosophy, medicine, ethics, cosmology, and human psychology. It was carried orally for centuries. It is as complex as the Greek canon, and older. But it does not appear on Dona Benta's bookshelf.
sections.For Young Readers:
Dona Benta's bookshelf is full of European stories — and they're wonderful stories. Don Quixote. Hercules. Peter Pan. But a bookshelf is a choice. Every book on the shelf is a book that was chosen, and every empty space is a story that was left out.

Nastácia's stories — from Africa, from Indigenous Brazil — are not on the shelf. Not because they're less important, but because the people who decided what counts as 'great literature' were usually European. They chose their own stories.

Imagine a bookshelf with room for everyone: Greek heroes AND Yoruba orixás. Peter Pan AND the Saci. Don Quixote AND the Turtle who outwits the Jaguar. That bookshelf doesn't exist yet in the Sítio. But we can build it.

illustrations.scene: Costumes and dress of Rio de Janeiro — European influence

items.name: When Nastácia Recognized Oxóssi in the Labyrinth

sections.Nastácia Tells:
The children went to Greece. With the Pó de Pirlimpimpim they went all the way to ancient Greece, to the labyrinth of the Minotaur and the twelve labors of Hercules.

They came home full of stories. Theseus in the labyrinth! The golden fleece! Diana the huntress in the forest with her bow!

I listened to Diana the huntress and I thought: Oxóssi.

Oxóssi — the hunter orixá, the lord of the forest, the one who never misses. In Yoruba tradition, Oxóssi is the one who feeds the village. His arrow is precise because hunger does not allow mistakes. He lives in the deepest part of the forest, where the animals are wildest and the medicine is strongest.

Diana hunts in the moonlight. Oxóssi hunts in the green dark of the mata. They are not the same. But they rhyme.

Hercules fought a lion with his bare hands. In Bahia, the capoeiristas fought slaveholders with their bare bodies — disguising a martial art as a dance so the masters wouldn't see the training.

Theseus entered the labyrinth with a thread to find his way back. Every enslaved person who escaped into the quilombo — the hidden communities in the forest — followed threads of knowledge passed mouth to mouth: which river to follow, which stars to read, which plants are safe.

I don't tell the children these parallels. Not yet. They're not ready to hear that their Greek heroes have cousins in the senzala. But someday, when they're old enough to sit with complicated truths, I'll tell them: every myth is a local version of a universal story. The Greeks had their version. We have ours. The only difference is who built a marble temple around it.
sections.What the Book Says: O Minotauro (1939) and Os Doze Trabalhos de Hércules (1944) are Lobato's Greek mythology adventures, where the Sítio children travel to ancient Greece and interact with mythological figures. These are celebrated as some of Lobato's most imaginative works.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Comparative mythology reveals that hunting deities, labyrinth journeys, and heroic trials appear in nearly every culture on earth. The Greek versions are not the originals — they are one branch of a much older tree. Yoruba mythology, Tupi-Guarani cosmology, and the traditions of the African diaspora contain parallel structures that are equally rich.

The colonization of mythology — the assumption that Greek myths are 'universal' while African myths are 'ethnic' — is one of the subtlest forms of cultural hierarchy. When Lobato sends his children to Greece but never to Ilê-Ifé (the Yoruba holy city), he's making a statement about whose myths count as civilization.
sections.For Young Readers:
Here's something amazing: almost every culture in the world has stories about heroes who fight monsters, enter dark places, and come back changed. The Greeks called their hero Theseus. The Yoruba have Ogum, the warrior who clears the path. The Tupi have warriors who face the jaguar.

These stories aren't copies of each other — they grew separately, like the same kind of flower growing in different gardens. They all say the same thing: life has dark passages, and courage means going through them.

When you read Greek myths, you're reading one garden's flowers. Nastácia's garden has its own flowers, just as beautiful. You just have to know where to look.

illustrations.scene: Playing capoeira or dance of war — spiritual martial art

items.name: When Everyone Shrank — The Prayer Nastácia Said

sections.Nastácia Tells:
In that story, Dona Benta finds a key that shrinks everyone in the world. Everyone becomes tiny. The whole earth changes.

I remember what I did when it happened. I prayed.

Not the Catholic prayer — not the Pai Nosso or the Ave Maria, though I know those too, and they are useful in their way. I prayed the prayer my mother taught me. The prayer you say when the world turns upside down and you don't know what's coming.

It's a prayer to Nanã — the oldest orixá, the grandmother of all the orixás, the one who was there before the world was formed. Nanã is mud. She is the swamp, the bottom of the river, the clay from which Oxalá shaped human beings. When everything falls apart, you go back to Nanã. You go back to the mud. You start over.

'Nanã Buruku, mãe da lama, mãe do começo — if the world is shrinking, let me shrink with my feet in your mud. Don't let me float. Don't let me lose the ground. Whatever size I am, let me be rooted.'

The children were terrified. The house was enormous around us. Insects became monsters. Everything we thought we understood about scale — about what is big and what is small, who matters and who doesn't — was undone.

And I thought: now you know what it feels like. To be small in a world made for someone bigger. To walk through a house where everything is scaled for another body. To be an afterthought in a story that someone else is writing.

Welcome, children. Welcome to my Tuesday.
sections.What the Book Says: A Chave do Tamanho (1942) is Lobato's most philosophical book. Dona Benta discovers a 'key' that reduces all humans to the size of insects. The adventure explores what would happen to human civilization at insect scale — ecology, society, and survival all change. It was written during World War II, and some readers see it as an allegory about the fragility of human systems.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Nanã Buruku is one of the least known orixás outside of Afro-Brazilian communities, but she is perhaps the most important. She is the primordial grandmother — the one who existed before creation, who provided the clay from which the first humans were shaped. Her domain is the swamp, the mud, the decomposing matter from which new life emerges.

Nanã is also the orixá of death and transformation — not the violent death of Ogum or the sudden death of Iansã, but the slow death of return to the earth. Composting. The breaking down of what was into what will be.

When the world shrinks, Nanã is the one to pray to — because she knows about smallness. She knows about the ground level. She has always been there, beneath everything, holding it up.
sections.For Young Readers:
Nanã (say: nah-NAH) is the grandmother of all the orixás in Yoruba tradition. She is connected to mud, swamps, and the earth itself. She represents the very beginning of things — the clay that the first people were made from.

When everything in your life feels like it's changing size — when things that seemed safe suddenly seem scary, or when you feel very small in a very big world — Nanã is the one who says: put your feet in the mud. Feel the ground. You are still here.

Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is remember that the ground is still under you, no matter how much everything else changes.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Black people going to church for baptism — spiritual syncretism

items.name: What Nastácia Knows You Cannot Reform

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Emília decided to reform nature. Of course she did. That doll thinks she can fix anything — she wanted to change the animals, rearrange the plants, improve what God and the orixás and the forest spirits spent millions of years creating.

She wanted fruit without seeds. Rivers that flow uphill. Animals without the parts she found ugly.

I listened from the kitchen and I shook my head.

Raizeira — my friend, the cabocla herb-woman — she told me once about the Kaingang people's way of seeing the world. She said the Kaingang don't divide nature into pretty and ugly, useful and useless. Everything has a place. The mosquito feeds the frog. The frog feeds the snake. The snake feeds the hawk. You cannot remove one piece without the whole web trembling.

'Reforming nature,' Raizeira said, 'is what people do when they think they're not part of it.'

Emília wants to reform nature because she's a doll. She was made, not born. She doesn't have roots in the soil. She doesn't bleed. She doesn't eat or sleep or age. She looks at nature from outside, like someone looking through a window, and she sees things she'd change.

But I — I am nature. My body is nature. The food I cook is nature becoming something else. The herbs I grow are nature offering itself. The prayers I pray are nature talking to nature.

You cannot reform what you are. You can only listen to it more carefully.
sections.What the Book Says: A Reforma da Natureza (1941) is a comic fantasy where Emília, given magical powers, tries to 'improve' nature by redesigning animals, plants, and natural processes. The results are disasters — everything she fixes creates new problems. Lobato uses the story to argue (gently) for the wisdom of natural systems.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The desire to 'reform nature' — to engineer ecosystems, redesign organisms, and improve on biological systems — is a recognizable colonial impulse. It assumes that nature is raw material awaiting human improvement. Indigenous cosmologies worldwide take the opposite view: nature is a community of persons (animals, plants, rivers, mountains) with agency, intelligence, and rights.

The Kaingang concept of ecological interdependence, the Yoruba understanding of axé (vital force flowing through all living things), and the Guarani reverence for the yvy marã'eỹ (the land without evil) all point to the same truth: reform is the wrong relationship. Reciprocity is the right one.

Lobato arrives at this conclusion through comedy. Indigenous and African traditions arrived there through millennia of relationship.
sections.For Young Readers:
Emília wants to fix nature — make fruit without seeds, rivers without mud, animals without the parts she doesn't like. But every 'fix' creates a new problem, because nature is a web where everything is connected.

Indigenous peoples have known this for thousands of years. The Kaingang people of southern Brazil understand that every animal, plant, and river has a role. Remove one piece and the whole system struggles.

The Yoruba tradition teaches something similar: everything in the world has axé (say: ah-SHEH) — a vital force, a life energy. Axé flows between all living things. When you try to 'reform' nature, you're trying to redirect a river that has been flowing for millions of years. Better to learn where it's going and walk alongside it.

illustrations.scene: Clearing of a forest — what cannot be reformed

items.name: Oil Beneath the Land

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Visconde de Sabugosa — the corn-cob intellectual, the man made of maize who thinks he's a European scientist — he found oil under the Sítio. Petróleo! The children were ecstatic. They imagined wealth, progress, the Sítio becoming a modern enterprise.

I was in the kitchen when they came running with the news. 'Tia Nastácia! There's oil under the farm!'

And I felt cold. Not excited. Cold.

Because I know what happens when people find things under the land. I know from my mother's stories about Bahia, about the gold mines of Minas Gerais where enslaved Africans dug until they died. I know from Raizeira's stories about what the bandeirantes did to the Guarani when they found the land was rich.

When you find treasure under the earth, the first thing that happens is: someone decides who the earth belongs to. And it's never the people who were already there.

The oil under the Sítio — Visconde calls it a geological discovery. I call it a wound waiting to happen. Because oil doesn't come up clean. It comes with displacement, with violence, with the assumption that what's under the ground belongs to whoever has the technology to extract it.

In Candomblé, we say: what's under the ground belongs to the ground. Nanã's domain. You can negotiate with her — but you ask first. You don't just drill.

Nobody asked the ground.
sections.What the Book Says: O Poço do Visconde (1937) is Lobato's most explicitly political book. Visconde discovers oil under the Sítio, and Lobato uses the story to advocate for Brazilian petroleum development — he was passionate about Brazil's oil sovereignty and critical of foreign oil companies. The book mixes children's adventure with economic nationalism.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Lobato's petroleum nationalism was progressive in one sense — he wanted Brazil, not foreign companies, to control Brazilian resources. But both positions (foreign extraction and national extraction) assume the same thing: that resources under the earth are there for human taking.

Indigenous land ethics across the Americas offer a different framework: the earth is not a container of resources. It is a relative. What lies beneath the surface — minerals, water, oil — is part of the earth's body. Extraction is surgery. You don't perform surgery without consent, without prayer, without a plan for healing.

The Yoruba concept of Ilê (earth/home/ground) and the Guarani concept of Yvy (earth/mother) both name the earth as a person. You can relate to a person. You don't extract from them.
sections.For Young Readers:
When people find something valuable under the ground — gold, oil, minerals — there's usually a rush to take it out. But what if the ground has feelings about that?

Many indigenous peoples believe it does. The earth is not just dirt and rock — it's alive, like a very large, very patient being. Taking things from deep underground is like taking something from someone's body. You have to ask. You have to give something back.

Lobato wanted Brazil to control its own oil. That was an important political idea. But Nastácia asks a deeper question: does anyone have the right to drill without asking the earth first?

illustrations.scene: Washing gold ore — mineral extraction from the land

items.name: The Girl Nobody Composted

sections.Nastácia Tells:
I need to tell you about Negrinha. Not the book — the girl.

Negrinha is a story Lobato wrote. Not a Sítio story. A different kind of story. The worst kind. The truest kind.

A seven-year-old orphan girl. 'Fusca, mulatinha escura' — dusky, dark little mulatta. She lives in the house of Dona Inácia, a woman who used to own slaves and never stopped acting like it. The whip was taken away by the Lei Áurea — the Golden Law of 1888 that ended slavery — but Dona Inácia found other tools. She found words. She found neglect. She found the terrible power of treating a child as furniture.

Negrinha is beaten. Negrinha is starved. Negrinha is told she is nothing — carne preta, black meat, a thing without a soul.

And then one day, visitors come with their daughters, and the daughters have dolls. Beautiful dolls. And they let Negrinha hold one. And for the first time in her life, Negrinha holds something soft and smiles.

And then the visitors leave, and the doll goes with them, and Negrinha dies. Of sadness. Of having tasted tenderness once and then having it taken away.

I cannot tell you this story without crying. I have cried over it so many times that the tears have become part of my cooking — I think sometimes the salt in my food is the salt of this girl's life.

Lobato wrote this story. The same man who called me 'macaca de carvão' wrote the most devastating portrait of anti-Black cruelty in Brazilian literature. How? How does the same hand write hate and compassion?

I'll tell you how: because Brazil is that hand. Brazil is the hand that whips and the hand that weeps and the hand that pretends the whipping and the weeping come from two different people.

Negrinha was never composted. She was never laid to rest in good soil where her suffering could feed new growth. She just died. In the story and in ten thousand real kitchens across Brazil, she just died. And nobody built a temple. Nobody sang a ponto. Nobody called her name in the gira and said: you were here. You mattered. You were a child and they stole your childhood and we remember.

So I'll do it now.

Negrinha. Eu te lembro. I remember you. You were here. You mattered. Your one moment of joy — holding that doll, feeling softness — that moment is sacred. It is an axé moment. It is the moment where the universe said: even in hell, there is light.

Rest now, minha filha. The ground is ready. We will plant something in your name.
sections.What the Book Says: Negrinha (1920) is one of the most important short stories in Brazilian literature. Lobato wrote it with savage empathy — an unflinching portrait of the cruelty that persisted after abolition. The story is deeply anti-racist in its content, even as its author held racist views in his personal life and other works. This contradiction is central to understanding Lobato.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The contradiction of Lobato — racist in life, anti-racist in his most powerful fiction — is not unique. It is the contradiction of Brazil itself: a country that gave the world samba, capoeira, and candomblé while maintaining one of the most brutal racial hierarchies in the Americas.

Negrinha represents the uncomposted trauma of Brazilian slavery. The Lei Áurea (1888) freed enslaved people but provided nothing: no land, no reparations, no education, no apology. Millions of people were simply released into a society that continued to treat them as less than human. Negrinha's story plays out every day in Brazil — in the favelas, in the domestic workers' quarters, in the statistics of Black children's mortality.

In Candomblé, when a spirit suffers and is not properly mourned, they become an egum — a restless dead. Negrinha is an egum. She needs a ceremony. She needs someone to say her name and pour water and light a candle. This grammar is, in part, that ceremony.
sections.For Young Readers:
This is a hard story. It's about a little girl who was treated very badly because of the color of her skin, in a time when slavery had just ended but the cruelty had not.

The most important thing about this story is not the cruelty — it's the moment when the girl holds a doll and smiles. That moment tells us: even when the world is at its worst, the human heart still reaches for tenderness. That reaching is sacred.

We remember Negrinha not to make ourselves sad, but to promise: never again. And to recognize that for many children in the world, 'never again' has not yet arrived.

illustrations.scene: Newly arrived Africans — displacement, suffering, dignity

items.name: When They Called Nastácia's People Lazy

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Lobato wrote about Jeca Tatu. The lazy caboclo. The rural Brazilian who squats by his hut and does nothing while the land around him rots. That's how Lobato saw the people of the countryside: as parasites, as urupes — toadstools growing on fallen trunks.

Jeca Tatu is not me. I'm not a cabocla — I'm Black, descended from Yoruba people who were brought to Bahia in chains. But Jeca's story and my story share a root: the colonizer looks at the colonized and says, 'Why don't you do anything? Why are you so poor? Why don't you improve?'

And the answer — the answer that Lobato eventually found, to his credit, years later — is: because you took everything. You took the land. You took the labor. You took the knowledge. And then you stand on the pile of what you took and ask us why we have nothing.

Lobato changed his mind about Jeca Tatu. Later in life, he wrote that Jeca wasn't lazy — he was sick, starved, abandoned by a government that built railroads for coffee exporters and left the rural poor to die of hookworm and malaria. That change of heart is important. It means even the man who created the insult could learn to see the truth underneath it.

But here's what Lobato never fully saw: Jeca Tatu was not just sick. He was robbed. The land Jeca squats on was stolen — from the Guarani, from the Kaingang, from the hundreds of indigenous peoples who lived on it before the bandeirantes came. And the labor that built the fazendas — that was stolen from people like my grandmother, who worked from before dawn until after dark and received nothing.

Jeca is not lazy. Jeca is looted. There's a difference.
sections.What the Book Says: Urupês (1918) is Lobato's breakthrough essay collection. The title essay introduces Jeca Tatu — a caricature of the rural Brazilian caboclo as ignorant, lazy, and parasitic. The essay was enormously influential and deeply controversial. Lobato later revised his view, attributing Jeca's condition to poverty and disease rather than inherent character.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The 'lazy native' is one of colonialism's oldest tools. It appears everywhere colonizers settled: in Africa (the 'lazy African'), in India (the 'indolent Indian'), in the Americas (the 'shiftless Indian,' the 'lazy Negro'). The logic is always the same: we took your land, your labor, your freedom — and now we blame you for having nothing.

Lobato's Jeca Tatu fits this pattern perfectly. The redemption — Lobato's later recognition that poverty, not character, explained Jeca's condition — is real but incomplete. It replaces moral contempt with medical paternalism. 'He's not bad, he's sick' is better than 'he's bad,' but it still doesn't ask: who made him sick? Who stole the land? Who built an economy that required his poverty?

Nastácia knows the answer because she lives it. She works eighteen-hour days in Dona Benta's kitchen. If she sat down and rested, she'd be called lazy too.
sections.For Young Readers:
Jeca Tatu is a character Lobato created to make fun of poor rural Brazilians. Later, Lobato realized that Jeca wasn't lazy — he was sick and poor because the system was unfair.

This is an important lesson: when someone seems to be doing nothing, ask why before you judge. Are they actually lazy? Or have they been given so little — so little land, so little food, so little opportunity — that doing nothing is what's left?

Nastácia works harder than anyone in the Sítio. She's the first one up and the last one to sleep. But in the books, she's treated as less important than a corn-cob philosopher and a rag doll. If that's not unfair, what is?

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Black porters carrying heavy loads — refuting laziness stereotypes

items.name: The Book Nastácia Found and Burned in Her Heart

sections.Nastácia Tells:
One day I was cleaning Dona Benta's study — dusting the shelves, straightening the books — and I found it. O Presidente Negro. The Black President.

A novel by Lobato. The man who writes the children's stories. The man whose characters I cook for.

I opened it. I read.

I read about a future where America elects a Black president — but it's not a story about hope. It's a story about race war. About eugenics. About a machine that straightens Black people's hair and lightens their skin so they can 'pass.' About white Americans so terrified of Black power that they develop a technology to sterilize Black women.

I closed the book. I put it back on the shelf. I went to the kitchen and I washed my hands three times.

I did not burn the book. Burning books is what the oppressor does. But I burned it in my heart. I let the rage turn to heat and the heat turn to ash and the ash fall into the soil where my faith lives.

This is the man who writes me. This is the man who decides what I say and how I move and whether I'm afraid of the Saci or not. This is the man who joined the São Paulo Eugenics Society and wrote letters to Renato Kehl about the 'improvement' of the Brazilian race.

And also: this is the man who wrote Negrinha. This is the man who gave Pedrinho the words 'Tia Nastácia é o povo.' This is the man who created Emília — the erê, the rebel, the one who refuses every hierarchy.

How do you hold both? How do you cook for a man who loves you and despises what you are?

You hold both the way you hold any contradiction in the terreiro: with both hands. You don't choose one truth and deny the other. You let them both burn. And in the ashes, you find what's yours.
sections.What the Book Says: O Presidente Negro (1926) is Lobato's only adult novel. It depicts a future America that elects a Black president, leading to racial panic and technological racial 'solutions' (hair-straightening rays, sterilization). The book is deeply eugenicist, reflecting Lobato's membership in the São Paulo Eugenics Society and his correspondence with eugenicists Renato Kehl and Artur Neiva. It is the most damning document in the case against Lobato.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
O Presidente Negro reveals what the Sítio books hide: the full architecture of Lobato's racial thinking. In the Sítio, racism appears as language — a slur here, a caricature there, deniable as 'the language of the time.' In O Presidente Negro, racism appears as system — a complete eugenic worldview where Black people are a problem to be solved through technology and elimination.

This is important because it means the slurs in the Sítio books are not accidents. They are symptoms of a deeper ideology. When Lobato calls Nastácia 'macaca de carvão,' he is not being carelessly rude. He is expressing — in children's literature — the same hierarchy that O Presidente Negro expresses in science fiction.

The man who wrote the children's books and the man who wrote the eugenic novel are the same man. Composting requires seeing the whole organism, not just the pretty leaves.
sections.For Young Readers:
Lobato wrote many kinds of books. Some were wonderful children's stories. One was a novel about race that contained very harmful ideas — ideas that said Black people were less than human and needed to be 'fixed.'

Why does this matter? Because it tells us something important: a person can create beautiful things and hold ugly beliefs at the same time. Knowing this doesn't mean we throw away the beautiful things. It means we read them with open eyes. We enjoy the adventure AND we notice when the adventure is being unfair.

Nastácia didn't burn the book. She read it, felt the hurt, and chose to keep going — to keep cooking, keep praying, keep telling her stories. That's not weakness. That's strength that looks forward.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Different Black nations — African identity and dignity

items.name: Going to Church on Sunday

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Every Sunday, I put on my white dress and I walk to the church in town. Dona Benta approves. 'Nastácia is a good Christian,' she tells the neighbors.

I am not a Christian.

Or rather: I am a Christian the way the river is a bridge. The water passes under the bridge, and the bridge thinks it contains the water. But the water was there first. The water will be there after the bridge falls.

I kneel in the church. I bate a cabeça — I touch my forehead to the ground. The priest sees devotion. Dona Benta sees faith. The other women see humility.

But here is what I'm really doing.

Beneath the floor of every church in Brazil — beneath the tile, beneath the stone, beneath the concrete — my ancestors buried their gods. When the Portuguese destroyed the terreiros and banned the ceremonies and whipped anyone caught honoring the orixás, my grandmother's people found a way. They carried the sacred stones — the otás, the vessels of the orixás — and they buried them beneath the church floors.

And then they went to Mass.

When I bate a cabeça in the church, I am not bowing to the crucifix. I am bowing to what's underneath. I am pressing my forehead to the floor where Xangô sleeps. Where Oxum dreams. Where Ogum waits with his iron patience.

The gesture looks Catholic. The gesture IS Yoruba.

This is how my people survived: by learning the colonizer's forms and filling them with our own meaning. The saints on the altar are not saints to me — they are masks. São Jorge is Ogum. Nossa Senhora is Oxum. Santa Bárbara is Iansã. The colonizers gave us their images and we placed our gods behind them, like paintings hung on a wall to hide the safe underneath.

Bater a cabeça means to touch the ground with your head. In Candomblé, you do this to greet the earth, to honor Nanã, to acknowledge that you came from the mud and will return to it. In the church, it looks like a Catholic genuflection. But the body knows the difference. The forehead knows who it's greeting.

Dona Benta thinks I'm praying to Jesus. I love Jesus — he was a man who washed fishermen's feet and got killed by the empire for it. But when I pray, I pray to the ground. I pray to what survived the crossing. I pray to the gods who traveled in chains and found a way to live beneath the floor of the master's church.

That is faith. Not the believing kind. The surviving kind.
sections.What the Book Says: In Lobato's books, Nastácia is portrayed as a devout Catholic who is also superstitious — she fears ghosts, makes the sign of the cross, and represents 'simple faith.' Lobato never explores the complexity of Afro-Brazilian religious syncretism or the possibility that Nastácia's faith is anything other than what it appears on the surface.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Religious syncretism in Brazil is one of the most remarkable acts of cultural resistance in human history. When the Portuguese colonizers banned African religious practices under penalty of death, enslaved Africans developed a system of correspondences: each Catholic saint was mapped to a Yoruba orixá. São Jorge (Saint George) became Ogum (the warrior orixá). Nossa Senhora da Conceição became Oxum (the mother of rivers). Santa Bárbara became Iansã (the orixá of storms).

This was not confusion or assimilation. It was strategy. The enslaved people attended Mass, venerated the saints, performed all the Catholic rituals — and underneath every gesture, they maintained their own faith intact. The Catholic form became a container for Yoruba content.

The practice of burying sacred otá stones (the vessels that hold an orixá's energy) beneath church floors is documented by historians and oral tradition alike. It is perhaps the most literal example of resistance hidden in plain sight: the colonizer's church, built on the colonized people's gods.

Bater a cabeça — the act of touching one's forehead to the ground — is a core gesture in Candomblé (called dobale or ikà). When performed in a Catholic context, it reads as humble prayer. When understood through its Yoruba origins, it is a greeting to the earth, to the ancestors, and to the orixás who live beneath the surface of things.
sections.For Young Readers:
When enslaved Africans in Brazil were forced to become Christians, they did something extraordinary: they said 'yes' on the outside and kept their own faith on the inside.

They matched each Catholic saint with one of their own gods (called orixás). Saint George became Ogum, the warrior spirit. The Virgin Mary became Oxum, the mother of rivers. The saints' paintings hung on the walls, but the real prayers went to the orixás behind them.

Some people buried sacred stones beneath church floors. Then, every Sunday, when they knelt to pray, they were actually greeting their own gods — the ones hiding right there, under the tile.

This is called syncretism (say: SIN-creh-tism). It's what happens when people find a way to keep their faith alive inside a system that's trying to destroy it. It's not lying. It's surviving. And the gods, Nastácia would say, always knew the difference.

illustrations.scene: Mass at the Church of Nossa Senhora da Candelária

items.name: The Herbs in the Kitchen Garden

sections.Nastácia Tells:
My kitchen garden. Dona Benta thinks it's for cooking. She's not wrong — everything I grow, I cook with. But every plant in that garden has two jobs: one for the body and one for the spirit.

Manjericão — basil. In the kitchen: pesto, sauces, beans. In the terreiro: it belongs to Oxalá, the orixá of creation. A bath of manjericão clears blocked paths.
