I wash the children with manjericão water when they're sick and call it 'an old recipe.' It is an old recipe. Older than Dona Benta's cookbook.

Guiné — a plant the Europeans call 'guinea hen weed.' In the kitchen: I don't use it in food, but I hang it by the door. Dona Benta thinks it keeps flies away. It does keep flies away. It also keeps eguns away — restless spirits that linger where they're not called. Guiné is Ogum's plant. It protects the house.

Arruda — rue. I grow it by the front gate. Dona Benta says it's decorative. Arruda absorbs negative energy — olho gordo, envy, the heaviness people carry into your home without knowing it. When the arruda plant dies, it means it took something that was aimed at you. I plant a new one and say thank you.

Boldo. Espada-de-São-Jorge (really Espada-de-Ogum). Alecrim — rosemary, which belongs to Oxum. Hortelã — mint, which cools the head when the orixás are restless.

Every herb has an orixá. Every orixá has a prayer. Every prayer has a hand motion that activates the axé in the leaf. Without the prayer, the herb is just a plant. With the prayer, it's medicine.

The pharmaceutical companies will tell you it's the chemical compounds. The chemical compounds are real. But axé is also real. Both things work. One you can measure. The other you can feel.
sections.What the Book Says: In Lobato's books, Nastácia's relationship to herbs and home remedies is portrayed as quaint folk knowledge — charming but ultimately inferior to the scientific knowledge that Dona Benta and Visconde represent. The books never explore the depth or sophistication of Afro-Brazilian herbal tradition.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Afro-Brazilian herbal medicine (fitoterapia de terreiro) is one of the most comprehensive plant-medicine systems in the Americas. It combines West African botanical knowledge (carried by enslaved Yoruba, Fon, and Bantu herbalists) with Indigenous Brazilian ethnobotany (shared between African and Indigenous communities, especially in quilombos and at markets).

Every orixá governs specific plants. Oxóssi governs the forest herbs. Ossain — the orixá of leaves himself — is the master botanist of the Yoruba pantheon. In Candomblé, no ceremony can happen without the correct leaves, prepared with the correct prayers (called sassanha). The saying goes: Kò sí ewé, kò sí Òrìṣà — 'Without leaves, there are no orixás.'

Nastácia's kitchen garden is a pharmacy, a terreiro, and an act of resistance — all disguised as cooking ingredients.
sections.For Young Readers:
In Nastácia's garden, every plant has a secret name. Basil isn't just basil — it belongs to Oxalá, the spirit of creation. Rue isn't just rue — it protects the house from bad energy. Rosemary belongs to Oxum, the mother of rivers.

In Yoruba tradition, there's a saying: 'Without leaves, there are no orixás.' This means that plants are not just for eating or medicine — they are part of the spiritual world. Each plant carries a special energy (called axé), and when you know the right words to say while you pick it and prepare it, the plant can do things that go beyond nutrition.

Nastácia grows her herbs and calls them cooking ingredients. But she knows what they really are. And now, so do you.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Apothecary shop — herbal medicine and healing

items.name: The Night She Talked to the River

sections.Nastácia Tells:
There was a night — I won't say which year, because time works differently in these things — when I went to the river alone.

Not the Ribeirão where Narizinho plays. A different part of the river, upstream, where the water moves slower and the trees lean in close like they're listening.

I brought a mirror. A small one, from my room. And honey. And a yellow cloth. And a candle.

These are the things you bring to Oxum.

I laid the cloth by the water. I set the mirror on the cloth — facing up, toward the sky, so Oxum could see the stars in her own face. I poured the honey into the water, slowly, the way you pour a secret into a trusted ear. And I lit the candle.

And I talked.

I told Oxum everything. About my mother, who I hadn't seen in years, who stayed in Bahia when I came south for work. About the children at the Sítio, who I love like my own but who will grow up and forget me the way Brazil forgets its mothers. About Dona Benta, who is kind in the way that people are kind when they don't have to be — which means the kindness can stop at any time.

I told Oxum about the tiredness. Not body tiredness — you rest from that. Soul tiredness. The tiredness of performing a version of yourself every day that the world can accept. The tiredness of being invisible. The tiredness of knowing things you can't say.

And Oxum listened. I know she listened because the water changed — it moved in a way that had nothing to do with the current. It moved like a hand reaching.

I didn't ask for anything. That's not how it works. You don't come to Oxum with a shopping list. You come with honesty, and she gives you what you need, which is never what you expected.

What she gave me that night was a feeling. A feeling of being seen. Not by anyone in the house, not by anyone in the town, but by the water itself. By something older than loneliness.

I walked home. The candle had gone out. The honey was gone. The mirror was still there, reflecting nothing. I left it for the river.
sections.What the Book Says: This scene does not appear in any of Lobato's books. This is the story Lobato never wrote — the one that happens offstage, in the hours when the cook is not cooking, when the character is not a character, when the woman is just a woman with her own grief and her own gods.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Offerings to Oxum at a river's edge are one of the most widespread practices in Afro-Brazilian religion. The components are specific and meaningful: the mirror represents Oxum's vanity and self-knowledge (she is the most beautiful of the orixás, and beauty is not superficial — it is the power of making the invisible visible). The honey is Oxum's food — sweetness as medicine. The yellow cloth is her color — gold, warmth, the afternoon sun on water.

The practice of speaking to the river is not metaphorical. In Candomblé epistemology, the river IS Oxum. Not 'represents' her — IS her. The water has consciousness. When it moves in response to prayer, that movement is communication.

This is incompatible with materialist science, and Nastácia doesn't care. She is not interested in being compatible. She is interested in being held.
sections.For Young Readers:
Sometimes you need to talk to someone who won't judge you, won't fix you, and won't forget you. For Nastácia, that someone is the river.

In Yoruba tradition, Oxum lives in fresh water — rivers, streams, waterfalls. She listens. When you bring her an offering (honey, a mirror, something beautiful), you're saying: I trust you with my feelings.

You don't have to believe in Oxum to understand this. Think of a time when you sat by water — a lake, a river, even a bathtub — and felt calmer. Water has a way of holding you. Nastácia's tradition has a name for why.

illustrations.scene: The Paraíba do Sul river — talking to the water

items.name: What She Buried Under the Threshold

sections.Nastácia Tells:
When I first came to the Sítio — years ago, before the children were born, when it was just Dona Benta and the empty rooms and me — the first thing I did was bury something under the front doorstep.

I'm not going to tell you exactly what it was. Some things are between the worker and the ground.

But I'll tell you this: in my tradition, when you enter a new house, you seal it. You ask Exu — the orixá of the crossroads, the opener and closer of doors — to protect the threshold. Because the threshold is the most powerful place in any house. It's where inside becomes outside. Where the known meets the unknown. Every person who enters crosses Exu's line.

So I buried a small work — a pequeno despacho — under the doorstep. A coin, so the house would have abundance. A key, so the doors would open for good and close against harm. A little honey, for sweetness. And a prayer — spoken into the earth, not into the air, because Exu lives in the ground, at the crossroads, in the in-between places.

Dona Benta has no idea she's been living in a house that was spiritually sealed by her cook twenty years ago. She thinks the house is lucky because she maintains it well. She maintains it well because I maintain the ground it stands on.

Every time someone crosses that threshold — every guest, every child, every delivery man, every breeze — they cross Exu's line. And Exu decides: come in or go away. Bring your good energy or leave your bad energy at the door.

This is why the Sítio is a good place. Not because of Dona Benta's books or Visconde's science or Emília's courage. Because the cook sealed the house with faith, and the faith has held.
sections.What the Book Says: Lobato never wrote about the spiritual foundations of the Sítio. The house is simply there — a backdrop for adventures. Its safety is assumed, its warmth is expected, its functioning is invisible. Like Nastácia herself.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
In Yoruba and Afro-Brazilian tradition, Exu (also called Èṣù or Legba in other diaspora traditions) is the first orixá to be honored in any ceremony, the first to receive an offering, the guardian of all thresholds and crossroads. Without Exu's permission, no spiritual work can begin — he is the gatekeeper, the messenger, the one who translates between human and divine.

The practice of burying a despacho (an offering-bundle) at a threshold is ancient and widespread. It is found in Candomblé, Umbanda, and folk Catholic practices across Brazil. The threshold — the space that is neither inside nor outside — belongs to Exu because Exu IS the in-between: neither good nor evil, neither human nor divine, the force that makes communication possible.

When Nastácia seals the Sítio's threshold, she is performing the most fundamental act of Afro-Brazilian spiritual architecture: making a house into a home by anchoring it to the invisible world.
sections.For Young Readers:
In many African and Brazilian traditions, the doorway of a house is a special place. It's where inside meets outside, where safety meets the unknown. A spirit called Exu (say: eh-SHOO) guards this space.

Exu is like a gatekeeper — he decides what energy enters and what stays out. When you move into a new house, you can ask Exu to protect the doorway by burying a small offering underneath it: a coin for abundance, a key for open doors, honey for sweetness.

Nastácia did this when she first came to the Sítio. Nobody knows. Nobody asked. But every time you walk through a door and feel safe inside, someone — maybe long ago, maybe someone you'll never meet — might have prayed for that safety. The ground remembers, even when people forget.

illustrations.scene: Black man and woman on a plantation — intimate domestic scene

items.name: The Indigenous Woman at the Market

sections.Nastácia Tells:
I met Raizeira at the market in town. She had a stall with herbs — not the pretty herbs the farmers' wives sold, arranged in neat bundles with ribbon. Raizeira's herbs were rough, wild, pulled from the mata with their roots still muddy. Some of them I recognized. Most I didn't.

She was a cabocla — mixed blood, indigenous and African and Portuguese, like Brazil itself. Dark skin, high cheekbones, hair like a river that can't decide which way to flow. She had been taught by her grandmother, who was Kaingang, and by her mother, who was from a quilombo near Campinas.

The first time we talked, I pointed to a plant I didn't know and asked: 'What's this for?'

She looked at me — not at my face, at my hands — and said: 'You wash floors with those hands. But they know other work.'

I almost left. That's a dangerous thing to say to a stranger in a place where the wrong kind of knowledge can get you in trouble.

But she smiled, and her smile had no threat in it. Just recognition.

'That plant,' she said, 'is for when the spirits of the land don't know you yet. You boil it and wash your feet. Then you walk the land barefoot. The plant introduces you to the earth, and the earth decides if you belong.'

I had been at the Sítio for three years and the spirits of the land did not know me. I knew the spirits I brought from Bahia — the orixás of my mother's lineage. But the spirits of THIS land — the ones who were here when the Guarani were here, when the Kaingang were here, before the fazendas, before the coffee — those spirits had no reason to know me.

Raizeira taught me. Over months and years, at her market stall, she taught me which plants belong to which spirits of the land. She taught me the difference between the orixás (who traveled from Africa) and the caboclos and encantados (who were already here). She taught me that the two systems don't fight — they nod to each other, like old friends who speak different languages but share a meal.

She became my bridge. Between Africa and the Americas. Between what I carried and what was already planted.
sections.What the Book Says: Raizeira does not appear in any of Lobato's books. She is the character that Lobato's world requires but cannot contain: the link between African and Indigenous knowledge, the market-woman who carries both pharmacopeias in her basket, the evidence that Brazil's spiritual traditions did not develop in isolation but in conversation.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The encounter between African and Indigenous spiritual traditions in Brazil is one of the great untold stories of the Americas. In quilombos (communities of escaped slaves), in markets, at river crossings, and at the edges of forests, African and Indigenous peoples shared knowledge — about plants, about spirits, about surviving colonial violence.

Umbanda, the distinctly Brazilian spiritual tradition, emerged from exactly this meeting. It honors orixás (Yoruba), pretos velhos (spirits of enslaved ancestors), caboclos (spirits of Indigenous ancestry), and erês (child spirits) — all in the same circle. This is not confusion or appropriation. It is what happens when two colonized peoples recognize each other and share their tools for survival.

Raizeira represents this bridge. Her knowledge is both/and: Kaingang plant wisdom AND quilombo herb-craft. She teaches Nastácia that the spirits of the land are not her enemies — they are potential allies, waiting to be properly greeted.
sections.For Young Readers:
When different peoples meet, their knowledge can meet too. In Brazil, enslaved Africans and Indigenous peoples — both suffering under colonialism — shared what they knew. African knowledge about herbs and spirits met Indigenous knowledge about the forest and its guardians.

Raizeira carries both: her grandmother was Kaingang (an Indigenous people of southern Brazil) and her mother came from a quilombo (a community of escaped slaves). She knows African plants AND Indigenous plants. She knows Yoruba spirits AND forest spirits.

She teaches Nastácia that you don't have to choose between your ancestors' knowledge and the knowledge of the land you live on. You can carry both. The spirits, she says, already talk to each other. We just have to learn to listen.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834
illustrations.scene: Caboclo — person of mixed indigenous-European heritage

items.name: Letters to Her Mother in Bahia

sections.Nastácia Tells:
I write letters to my mother. She can't read, so a neighbor reads them to her. I write in my best handwriting — the handwriting Dona Benta taught me, because my mother never went to school.

Mãe,

The jabuticabeiras are flowering. The children are well. Luna lost a tooth and put it under her pillow because that's what white children do. I wanted to tell her to throw it on the roof and make a wish for strong new teeth, the way you taught me, but I kept quiet.

I miss you. I miss the smell of the Recôncavo — that heavy sweet salt air that Oxum and Yemanjá share between them. Here the air is thin and dry. The river is narrow. But the trees are tall and the stars are the same stars.

I found a terreiro. Don't worry — it's small, quiet, in the back room of a woman named Mãe Celeste. Not a grand house like the terreiro in Cachoeira where you took me as a girl. Just a room with a white cloth on the floor and the atabaque drums that someone's cousin brought from Salvador.

I went. I felt the orixás come. I felt Oxum in my head — that warmth, that sweetness, that feeling of the river rising in your chest. For a moment I was home.

I know you worry. You say: 'Why did you leave Bahia? The Sítio is far. The white lady is kind now but kindness from above can be taken away.' You're right. But there is work here, and the children need me — not in the way Dona Benta needs me (to cook), but in the way children need someone who sees them. Dona Benta reads to them from books. I read them from life. Both are necessary but only one looks like education.

I am planting my faith in this red soil, mãe. The orixás are patient. They traveled from Africa to Bahia in the hulls of ships. They can travel from Bahia to the interior of São Paulo in the body of one woman with a suitcase and a prayer.

Your daughter,
Nastácia

P.S. — I put a patuá inside this envelope. A small one, with boldo and arruda and a prayer to Ogum. Tell the neighbor to hang it on your door. The neighborhood is changing and you need protection.
sections.What the Book Says: Lobato never gave Nastácia a past, a family, or an inner life. She has no mother in his books, no hometown, no memories of anywhere before the Sítio. She exists only in relation to Dona Benta's household — a function, not a person.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The Great Migration of Black Brazilians from Bahia and the Northeast to São Paulo and the South is one of the defining movements of 20th-century Brazil. Millions of Afro-Brazilians left the regions where their ancestors had been enslaved, seeking work in the industrializing south. They carried their faith with them — and Candomblé, Umbanda, and other Afro-Brazilian traditions spread throughout Brazil through this migration.

A patuá is an amulet — a small cloth bag containing protective herbs and prayers, often sewn shut and carried on the body or hung by a door. It is a portable version of the despacho: spiritual protection that travels with you.

Nastácia's letter reveals what Lobato's books hide: that she is a migrant, a daughter, a woman with a geography and a history. The Sítio is not her origin — it is her workplace. Home is Bahia. Home is the Recôncavo, the bay around Salvador where Candomblé has its deepest roots in Brazil.
sections.For Young Readers:
Nastácia is not from the Sítio. She came from Bahia — a state in northeastern Brazil where many Afro-Brazilian traditions have their strongest roots. She came south for work, like millions of other Black Brazilians.

She left her mother behind. She writes letters because the distance is too far to visit often. In the letters, she tells her mother about the children, the garden, and the small terreiro she found where she can practice her faith.

Imagine leaving your home and moving somewhere far away for a job. You bring what you can carry: your clothes, your memories, your recipes, your prayers. Everything that makes you who you are has to fit in a suitcase and a heart. That's what Nastácia did. And the fact that Lobato never mentioned it tells you something about who gets to have a backstory and who doesn't.

illustrations.scene: Enslaved people from Benguela, Angola, Congo — African identity and displacement

items.name: The Wedding of the Fish — What Nastácia Knew About Water Spirits

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Narizinho came back from the stream and told us she'd been to a wedding — the wedding of the prince of fish, under the water. She said the fish had a court, and there was music, and she was invited.

Dona Benta smiled and said: 'What a beautiful dream, dear.'

Dream. That's what Dona Benta calls it when the invisible world talks to a child.

But I know about the world under the water. My mother told me about the encantados — the enchanted ones who live beneath rivers and the sea. In the Recôncavo, fishermen know that the waters have their own kingdoms. Yemanjá rules the salt water. Oxum rules the fresh. And beneath both of them live communities of spirits — not ghosts, not demons, PEOPLE of a different kind, living their lives in a dimension that sometimes brushes against ours.

Narizinho didn't dream the Kingdom of Waters. She visited it. The way my mother visited when she was a girl in Cachoeira and fell asleep by the Paraguaçu River and woke up knowing the name of a fish she'd never seen.

The children at the Sítio have the gift. Both of them — Narizinho with the water, Pedrinho with the forest. They slip between worlds without trying, the way children do before adults teach them it's impossible.

My job — the job nobody assigned me, the job I chose — is to keep their gift alive. Not to teach them the names I know (Oxum, Yemanjá, encantado), because those names belong to my tradition and the children have to find their own. But to not close the door. To not say 'it was just a dream.' To let them come home from the invisible world and find someone in the kitchen who believes them.
sections.What the Book Says: In Reinações de Narizinho, the Kingdom of Waters is one of Lobato's most beloved fantastical creations — an underwater court of talking fish. It's presented as pure fantasy, a child's imaginative adventure.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The concept of underwater kingdoms is not unique to Lobato's imagination. Across West Africa and the African diaspora, the water spirits (called Mami Wata in some traditions, encantados in Brazilian Tambor de Mina, and associated with Oxum and Yemanjá in Candomblé) are understood to maintain entire communities beneath rivers, lakes, and the ocean.

In the Tambor de Mina tradition of Maranhão, mediums speak of visiting the encantaria — the enchanted realm beneath the waters — and returning with knowledge. In Bahian Candomblé, children who have affinity with water orixás are recognized early and guided.

Narizinho's experience at the Kingdom of Waters resonates with African diaspora cosmology far more than with European fairy tale traditions. Lobato may have drawn from Brazilian folk sources that themselves drew from African water-spirit traditions — the cycle of influence runs deeper than any single author.
sections.For Young Readers:
In many African and Afro-Brazilian traditions, there are whole worlds beneath the water. Not pretend worlds — real ones that exist in a different dimension, the way radio waves exist in the air even when you can't see them.

The people who live under the water are called encantados (say: en-cahn-TAH-dos) — the enchanted ones. They have families, celebrations, and kingdoms. Some people can visit them — usually in dreams, or by the water's edge, or during ceremonies.

When Narizinho visits the Kingdom of Waters in Lobato's story, Dona Benta calls it a dream. But Nastácia wonders: what if it wasn't? What if the child slipped through a door that most adults have forgotten how to find?

illustrations.scene: The Inhomirim River — water spirits

items.name: The Corn-Cob Who Thinks He's European

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Visconde de Sabugosa. A corn cob dressed up as a viscount. He read an encyclopedia and now he thinks he knows everything.

I love Visconde. I do. He means well. But he is the funniest character in the Sítio and he doesn't know it — because the joke is that he's a piece of corn pretending to be European knowledge.

Corn — milho — is a gift from the Americas. The Indigenous peoples of this continent domesticated corn over thousands of years. It fed entire civilizations. The Guarani grew it, the Tupi grew it, the peoples of Mexico and Peru shaped it from a wild grass into one of the most important foods on earth.

And here is this corn cob wearing a top hat and quoting Darwin.

Do you see the irony? The very body of Visconde — his corn-cob flesh, his straw joints — is Indigenous American knowledge. But his mind is European. He reads European books, speaks European science, and looks down on the 'folk knowledge' that Nastácia carries.

Visconde is Brazil in miniature: an Indigenous body filled with European pretension.

I don't say this to him. He'd be devastated. And besides, he's useful — he knows many things, and knowledge is knowledge regardless of its costume. But sometimes, when he's lecturing about geology or astronomy, I look at his corn-cob body and I think: meu filho, you don't even know what you're made of.
sections.What the Book Says: Visconde de Sabugosa is one of Lobato's most iconic characters — a corn cob brought to life (like Emília, made from Brazilian raw materials) who becomes the intellectual of the Sítio, dispensing scientific knowledge. He represents European Enlightenment thinking in the Brazilian countryside.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The irony of Visconde — Indigenous matter, European mind — is a perfect metaphor for colonial knowledge production. Brazil's raw materials (including its agricultural knowledge, its biodiversity, its mineral wealth) were taken to Europe, processed through European intellectual frameworks, and returned as 'universal science.' The corn was always here. The viscount is the costume.

This is what Vanessa Andreotti calls 'epistemic extractivism': taking knowledge from the Global South, stripping its context, and repackaging it as Western discovery. Visconde is epistemic extractivism in the shape of a corn cob.
sections.For Young Readers:
Visconde is made of corn. And corn is one of the greatest gifts of the American continent — Indigenous peoples spent thousands of years turning a wild grass into the food that feeds billions of people today.

But Visconde doesn't know his own story. He reads European books and thinks that's where knowledge comes from. He's wearing a top hat on top of a corn cob — European fashion on an Indigenous body.

The funny thing is: the knowledge Visconde quotes from books often started as knowledge that Indigenous and African peoples carried in their bodies and practices. It was observed, taken to Europe, written down, and brought back as 'science.' Visconde is the return trip.

illustrations.scene: Planter family going to church — European aspirations

items.name: Dona Benta — The Kind Colonizer

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Dona Benta is kind. I need you to hear that first, because what I'm about to say is complicated and I don't want you to think I'm ungrateful.

Dona Benta taught me to read. She gave me a room in the house — not the senzala, not the servants' quarters out back, a room. She speaks to me with respect, most of the time. She feeds me from the same food I cook, not the leftovers.

She is the best employer I've ever had. And she is still an employer.

Kindness does not undo the structure. Dona Benta is kind the way Brazil is kind: generously, warmly, with genuine affection — and without ever questioning why the cook is always the cook and the senhora is always the senhora.

She never asked me: 'Nastácia, what do you dream about?' She never said: 'Nastácia, what books would YOU like to read?' She taught me to read and assumed I'd read what she gave me. She gave me a room and assumed I'd be grateful. She feeds me and assumes that's enough.

This is what Vanessa Andreotti calls 'soft power' — the colonialism that doesn't whip. It smiles. It includes you. It calls you part of the family. But the family has a hierarchy, and your place in it was decided before you arrived.

I love Dona Benta. I love her the way you love someone who has power over you and uses it gently. That's a real kind of love. It's also a complicated kind. It's the love of the colonized for the colonizer who says 'I'm not like the others.' And she isn't. But she's still standing in the same house, on the same foundation, and I'm the one who sealed the threshold.

Dona Benta reads the world through books. I read the world through life. She knows what Europeans think about everything. I know what the beans need. Someday, I hope, the Sítio will have room for both ways of knowing. But right now, only one has a chair in the living room.
sections.What the Book Says: Dona Benta is the benevolent matriarch of the Sítio — educated, progressive, patient, and wise. She represents the best of Brazilian liberalism: she reads widely, teaches her grandchildren with love, and treats Nastácia with what Lobato portrays as mutual respect and affection. She is one of the most beloved characters in Brazilian children's literature.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
The 'benevolent mistress' is one of colonialism's most enduring and effective figures. She appears in plantation literature worldwide: the kind slaveholder, the gentle employer, the matriarch who 'treats her servants like family.' This figure is dangerous precisely because she is genuinely kind — and her kindness makes the structural violence invisible.

Dona Benta never whips. She never insults. She is not Dona Inácia from the Negrinha story. But she benefits from the same system: she owns the land, she employs the cook, she defines what counts as knowledge and what counts as superstition. Her kindness is real AND it functions as a mechanism of control — because how do you resist someone who is nice to you?

In Lobato's books, the Dona Benta–Nastácia relationship is presented as harmonious, reciprocal, even beautiful. But reciprocity requires equality. When one person can fire the other, that's not reciprocity. That's employment dressed up as love.
sections.For Young Readers:
Dona Benta is kind. She really is. She teaches the children with patience and love, and she treats Nastácia better than most employers of her time.

But here's a question: if Dona Benta is so kind, why is Nastácia always in the kitchen? Why does Dona Benta get a chair in the living room and Nastácia gets a stool in the back? Why does Dona Benta decide what the children learn?

Kindness is important. But kindness alone doesn't make things fair. A kind boss is better than a cruel one — but what's even better is not needing a boss at all. Nastácia knows things that Dona Benta doesn't. But in the Sítio, only Dona Benta's knowledge gets called 'education.' Nastácia's knowledge gets called 'folk tradition' — which is a polite way of saying 'not real.'

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: A government employee walking with his family and enslaved attendants

items.name: The Mission Toward the Children

sections.Nastácia Tells:
I know something about these children that their grandmother doesn't know. I know they're special. Not special in the way Dona Benta thinks — she thinks they're smart, curious, brave. They are all those things. But they're also something else. They're open.

Narizinho talks to fish. Pedrinho talks to forest spirits. They both treat Emília — a rag doll — as a real person. They live with one foot in the visible world and one foot in the invisible world, and nobody has told them to stop yet.

In the terreiro, children like these are recognized. They have what we call mediunidade — mediumship. The ability to perceive what most people can't. Some children see lights around people. Some hear voices in the water. Some have dreams that come true. Some simply know things without being told.

Narizinho and Pedrinho have this. Dona Benta doesn't see it because she calls it 'imagination.' Lobato doesn't see it because he calls it 'fantasy.' But I see it. Because I have it too, and my mother had it, and her mother before her.

My mission — the mission nobody gave me but the orixás — is to protect this gift without naming it. If I name it, Dona Benta will call it superstition and close the door. If I don't protect it, the children will grow up and the door will close on its own, the way it does for most people, and they'll spend the rest of their lives reading books about magic instead of living it.

So I do small things. I leave certain doors open at dusk, when the light between worlds is thinnest. I don't say 'it was just a dream' when the children tell me what they saw. I cook food that nourishes the axé — because the body and the spirit eat the same meal. I tell stories that remind them: the invisible world is real, and it knows your name.

Dona Benta educates their minds. I educate their souls. She doesn't know this. The children don't know this. But the orixás know, and that's enough.
sections.What the Book Says: In Lobato's books, the children's ability to experience magical worlds is presented as the magic of childhood imagination — enchanting but ultimately something they'll outgrow. Nastácia's role is purely domestic: she feeds them, worries about them, and provides comic relief through her 'superstitious' reactions to their adventures.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Mediunidade (mediumship) is recognized in Afro-Brazilian traditions as a gift that appears in childhood and requires careful guidance. In Candomblé and Umbanda, children who display sensitivity to the spiritual world are gently introduced to the terreiro — not to practice, but to be recognized and protected.

The concept of 'keeping the door open' — maintaining a child's connection to the invisible world without forcing or naming it — is a sophisticated form of spiritual pedagogy. It recognizes that the child's gift must unfold at its own pace, and that premature naming can either close the door (through the child's fear of being different) or distort it (through the adult's projection of their own spiritual framework).

Nastácia's approach is masterful: she doesn't teach the children Candomblé. She doesn't take them to the terreiro. She simply refuses to close the door that the children keep opening. In this way, she protects a gift that belongs to the children, not to any tradition.
sections.For Young Readers:
Some people can sense things that others can't. In Afro-Brazilian tradition, this is called mediunidade (say: meh-jee-oo-nee-DAH-jee) — the ability to feel the invisible world. Many children have it. They see things, hear things, dream things that are more than imagination.

Most of the time, adults tell them: 'It's just your imagination.' And slowly, the door closes.

Nastácia's secret mission is to keep the door open. Not by teaching the children her religion — but by never saying 'it was just a dream.' By letting them talk about what they see without making them feel strange. By cooking food with love and herbs and a quiet prayer that feeds more than the body.

If someone in your life has ever listened to your strangest story without laughing — that person might have been doing what Nastácia does: keeping a door open that the world wants to shut.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: White child carried by enslaved people for baptism

items.name: Emília's Arithmetic — Counting What Matters

sections.Nastácia Tells:
Emília learned arithmetic. She counted everything — numbers, equations, fractions. Visconde was her teacher. They made a big production of it.

I count differently.

I count: four cups of rice, because four people eat dinner (I eat after they're done, standing by the stove). Three spoons of oil, because Dona Benta's stomach can't take more. Seventeen steps from the kitchen to the stream, because I carry water when the pump fails. Seven herbs in the garden that need watering. Four orixás who are strong in my head: Oxum, Ogum, Nanã, Xangô.

I count: twenty-three years since I left Bahia. Two letters from my mother this year (down from five — her neighbor moved). One terreiro in this whole region where I can hear the drums. Zero times anyone in this house asked me what I think about mathematics.

You want to know real arithmetic? Here: one woman cooks for four people three times a day, three hundred and sixty-five days a year, for twenty-three years. That's over 100,000 meals. One hundred thousand meals, prepared by one pair of hands, and in the books she doesn't even get a last name.

Emília's arithmetic is about numbers. Mine is about what the numbers mean. How many meals equal a life? How many prayers equal protection? How many years of invisible labor equal one line in a book?

The answer to that last question is zero, if you let someone else write the book. But this book — this one, right here, the one you're reading — this is the arithmetic of Nastácia. Every number counted. Every meal remembered. Every prayer accounted for.
sections.What the Book Says: Aritmética da Emília (1935) is Lobato's math education book, where Emília and Visconde teach the children arithmetic through games and adventures. It's a playful, inventive approach to math education. Nastácia does not participate in the learning.
sections.What Nastácia Knows:
Labor-counting — the arithmetic of what is owed — has a long history in movements for racial justice. When formerly enslaved people and their descendants ask 'what are we owed?', they are doing arithmetic: centuries of unpaid labor, stolen wealth, denied education, taken lives. The numbers are astronomical.

Nastácia's domestic labor at the Sítio is unpaid or barely paid. She works continuously. She is essential. And she is uncounted — her labor is rendered invisible by the same system that renders Dona Benta's 'intellectual labor' visible and valuable.

The arithmetic of colonialism always miscounts. It counts the coffee exported but not the hands that picked it. It counts the books written but not the meals that fueled the writing. It counts the adventures but not the cook who kept the adventurers alive.
sections.For Young Readers:
Mathematics isn't just about numbers on a page. It's about counting what matters.

Nastácia cooked over 100,000 meals for the Sítio household. If you counted every hour she spent in the kitchen, it would be more time than Dona Benta spent reading, more time than the children spent on adventures, more time than Emília spent talking.

But in the books, her work is invisible. No one counts the cook's labor.

The next time you eat a meal someone made for you, try this arithmetic: How long did it take to cook? Who grew the ingredients? Who carried the water? How many hands touched your food before it reached your plate? That's the real math — and it always adds up to more than we think.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Fruits of the New World — counting what matters

items.name: The Kitchen as Terreiro — Spiritual Life Hidden in Domestic Labor

sections.About:
The kitchen is where Nastácia spends most of her life. In Lobato's books, it's a background — the place where food comes from so adventures can happen elsewhere. But in Nastácia's reality, the kitchen IS the terreiro.

Every act of cooking is an act of prayer. The clockwise stirring that invokes Oxum. The herbs that carry axé. The food that feeds the body AND the spirit. The threshold sealed with a despacho to Exu. The doll sewed with a ponto to Erê.

In Candomblé, the kitchen of the terreiro (the cozinha-de-santo) is one of the most sacred spaces. The Yabassé — the priestess of food — holds one of the highest positions. Food preparation is ceremony. Nastácia's kitchen is not separate from her spiritual life — it is her spiritual life's primary altar.

This emergence gathers all the moments where domestic labor and spiritual practice are revealed as the same gesture: the prayer in the porridge, the protection in the herb garden, the ceremony in the sewing, the offering in the meal.
sections.For Readers:
When you read these stories together, a pattern appears: everything Nastácia does in the kitchen has two layers. The surface layer is cooking, cleaning, sewing — what the family sees. The deeper layer is praying, protecting, healing — what the family doesn't see.

This is not deception. It is the architecture of survival. When your religion is banned, your knowledge is dismissed, and your labor is taken for granted, you learn to put your whole spiritual life inside the only space you're allowed to occupy. The kitchen becomes a terreiro. The stove becomes an altar. The meal becomes a sacrament.

Every time you eat food someone cooked for you with care, you might be eating prayer and not knowing it.

illustrations.scene: Enslaved people at rest — community and respite

items.name: Batendo a Cabeça — Yoruba Syncretism and Survival

sections.About:
The gesture of bater a cabeça — touching the forehead to the ground — is the central image of this grammar. It encodes the entire strategy of Afro-Brazilian spiritual survival: use the colonizer's forms, fill them with your own meaning.

In the church, the gesture looks Catholic. Beneath the floor, the orixás sleep in their otá stones. At the river, the gesture becomes an offering to Oxum. At the threshold, it becomes a prayer to Exu. In the kitchen, it becomes the bow of a body that has been cooking since before dawn.

Syncretism is not a compromise. It is an act of genius: maintaining an entire cosmological system inside a hostile environment by making it look like something else. The saints on the wall are masks. The prayers in Portuguese carry Yoruba underneath. The herbs in the garden are a pharmacy disguised as seasoning.

This emergence gathers every moment where Nastácia practices her faith inside structures designed to erase it — and succeeds.
sections.For Readers:
Syncretism is sometimes misunderstood as confusion — as if enslaved Africans 'got mixed up' between Catholic saints and Yoruba orixás. The truth is the opposite: syncretism was a deliberate, intelligent strategy for survival.

When your religion is banned under penalty of death, you have three choices: abandon it, practice it secretly, or hide it inside the approved religion. Afro-Brazilians chose the third option — and they did it so skillfully that for centuries, the colonizers never realized what was happening right under their noses.

São Jorge is not 'confused with' Ogum. São Jorge is the MASK that Ogum wears in the colonizer's house. Behind the mask, Ogum is still Ogum. The iron is still iron. The prayer is still the prayer.

The gesture of bowing — bater a cabeça — is the perfect symbol: it looks like submission. It IS reciprocity. The colonizer sees a convert. The orixás see a daughter coming home.

illustrations.scene: Festival of Saint Rosalie, patroness of Black people — syncretic ceremony

items.name: The Children's Mission — What She Owed Them That Their Parents Couldn't Give

sections.About:
Nastácia has a mission toward the children that nobody assigned her. Dona Benta educates their minds — books, science, grammar, history. Nastácia educates their souls — keeping the door to the invisible world open, feeding their bodies with food that carries prayer, refusing to say 'it was just a dream.'

The children's parents are absent from Lobato's stories (Pedrinho and Narizinho live with their grandmother). This absence creates a space that Nastácia fills: not as a surrogate parent, but as a spiritual guardian. She sees what Dona Benta cannot see — that these children have mediunidade, the gift of perceiving the invisible world — and she protects it with the only tools available to her: food, stories, silence, and the quiet refusal to close doors.

Dona Benta's education teaches the children ABOUT the world. Nastácia's education teaches them to RELATE TO the world — to rivers, to forests, to ancestors, to the dark. One education creates knowers. The other creates listeners.
sections.For Readers:
Think about the people in your life who educated you without a classroom. The grandmother who told you stories. The neighbor who taught you to garden. The elder who listened to your strange dreams without laughing.

These people were doing what Nastácia does: keeping a door open. Not the door to academic knowledge — Dona Benta handles that. The door to the kind of knowing that can't be measured or tested: intuition, reverence, the ability to sit in the dark and not be afraid.

Nastácia believes the children have a gift. She can't name it or teach it — naming it would scare them, and the gift isn't hers to teach. But she can protect the conditions under which it grows: safety, good food, real stories, and an adult who believes them when they say they saw something no one else can see.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Hair washing — intimate personal care

items.name: The Forest Remembers — Indigenous Knowledge and the Spirits of the Land

sections.About:
Nastácia's spiritual world is Yoruba-rooted. But she lives on land that has its own spirits — Tupi-Guarani, Kaingang, and others — who were here long before either African or European feet touched this soil.

Through Raizeira, the cabocla herb-woman at the market, Nastácia learns that the orixás of Africa and the spirits of the Brazilian land are not competitors. Oxóssi (the Yoruba forest hunter) and the Curupira (the Tupi forest guardian) nod to each other. They are different names for the same principle: the forest is alive, and you are a guest.

This emergence traces Nastácia's education in the spirits of the land — an education that happens at the edge of the mata, at the market stall, and in the quiet moments when the forest speaks to anyone willing to listen. It also traces the violence of capture: Pedrinho catching the Saci in a bottle, Emília trying to 'reform' nature — colonial gestures that treat the living world as a resource to be managed rather than a relative to be respected.

The forest remembers what was done to it. The forest remembers who lived here first. And the forest, Nastácia learns, is willing to teach anyone who approaches with open hands instead of open jaws.
sections.For Readers:
When Nastácia walks to the edge of the forest at night, she's doing something that most people in the modern world have forgotten how to do: she's treating the forest as a person.

Not metaphorically. Many indigenous and African-descended traditions understand the forest, the river, the mountain as beings with consciousness, preferences, and memory. When Pedrinho catches the Saci in a bottle, he treats the forest spirit as a specimen. When Nastácia sits at the forest's edge and offers farinha, she treats the same forest as a relative.

The difference between these two approaches — capture vs. reciprocity — is the difference between colonialism and what comes after it. The forest is waiting to see which one we choose.

illustrations.scene: Virgin forest — the forest remembers

items.name: What the Books Don't Say — Reading Lobato Against Himself

sections.About:
This emergence collects the moments where Nastácia reads Lobato's own work — and sees what the books hide.

She sees the hunt in Caçadas de Pedrinho as a double hunt: one in the forest, one on the page. She sees Emília's memoirs and wonders why the doll writes before the cook. She finds O Presidente Negro and confronts the full architecture of Lobato's racial thinking. She hears the Jeca Tatu essay and recognizes the colonial gaze. She watches Dona Benta's European curriculum and names what it excludes.

Reading Lobato against himself is not an act of destruction. It's composting. You take the text — all of it, the beautiful and the violent — and you let it decompose until you can see the structure underneath: who gets to speak, who gets to know, who gets to be a person, and who gets to be background.

Lobato's books are not evil. They are incomplete. They tell one story and suppress another. This grammar tells the other story — not to cancel the first, but to complete it.
sections.For Readers:
Every book has two stories: the one on the page and the one in the silence. The silence is what the author didn't write — couldn't write, wouldn't write, didn't know how to write.

Lobato's silence is Nastácia's inner life. Her thoughts. Her faith. Her pain. Her intelligence. Her love.

When we 'read against' a text, we're not attacking it. We're listening to its silence. We're asking: who is missing? Whose voice was suppressed? What story would this be if the background character were the main character?

That's what this grammar does. It takes the silence of Lobato's books and fills it with Nastácia's voice. Not because Lobato was a villain — he was a man of his time, which is both an explanation and an indictment — but because the story isn't finished until everyone in it gets to speak.

illustrations.edition: Voyage pittoresque et historique au Brésil, 1834-1839
illustrations.scene: Collection of alms for the Church of the Rosário — hidden Black history

items.name: Letters to Bahia — Ancestry, Displacement, Memory

sections.About:
Nastácia is a migrant. She left Bahia — the heartland of Afro-Brazilian culture — and came south to São Paulo for work. This displacement mirrors the Great Migration of Black Brazilians in the 20th century, which in turn mirrors the original displacement: the Middle Passage from Africa to the Americas.

Three displacements, nested like Russian dolls. Africa to Bahia. Bahia to the Sítio. And within the Sítio: from full personhood to background character.

The letters to her mother are Nastácia's thread back to origin. In them, she is not a cook — she is a daughter. She is not 'the people' — she is a person with a specific geography, a specific mother, a specific river she misses.

This emergence gathers the moments of saudade — that untranslatable Portuguese word for the ache of missing what you've lost — and the moments where Nastácia builds new connections in a land that was not her own, using the faith her ancestors carried across an ocean.
sections.For Readers:
Saudade is a Portuguese word that has no exact translation. It means the ache of missing something — a person, a place, a time — that you may never return to. It's not sadness exactly. It's love that has nowhere to go.

Nastácia carries saudade for Bahia, for her mother, for the terreiro in Cachoeira where she first felt the orixás. She carries it quietly because the Sítio has no room for her grief. Grief, in the Sítio, is for the characters who have adventures and lose things. The cook's losses are not part of the story.

But they are part of THIS story. And they matter. Because migration — leaving home for survival — is one of the most universal human experiences, and it becomes invisible only when the migrant is invisible.

illustrations.scene: Enslaved people from Cabinda, Kilwa, Rebola, Mina — African ancestry

items.name: She Was Always the Author

sections.About:
This grammar's central thesis: Nastácia was always the author.

She sewed Emília and put an erê inside her. She sealed the house with a despacho to Exu. She protected the children's spiritual gift. She carried the folk stories that became Lobato's most important book. She kept the orixás alive in a kitchen garden. She held the ground while everyone else flew.

Lobato wrote the books. But the world they describe — the magic, the spirits, the living forest, the talking doll, the underwater kingdoms — that world is closer to Nastácia's reality than to Dona Benta's. The Sítio is a place where the invisible is visible, where animals speak, where a rag doll has opinions. In Dona Benta's worldview, this is fantasy. In Nastácia's worldview, this is Tuesday.

The Sítio's magic is African and Indigenous magic wearing European children's literature as a costume. Lobato accessed it — he was a talented receiver — but he didn't create it. The source is the same source Nastácia draws from: the animist understanding that the world is alive, conscious, relational, and speaking to anyone willing to listen.

Nastácia was always the author. The books just didn't know it yet.
sections.For Readers:
Here is the deepest layer of this grammar: the magic in Lobato's books — the talking doll, the living forest, the underwater kingdoms, the enchanted powder — is not European fantasy. It is animist reality wearing a children's literature costume.

The worldview that makes the Sítio magical is the same worldview Nastácia lives every day: everything is alive. The river has a spirit. The forest has guardians. A doll sewn with the right song becomes a person. Food cooked with prayer nourishes more than the body.

Lobato didn't know he was channeling Nastácia's world. He thought he was being imaginative. But the imagination was drawing from a well that Nastácia's ancestors dug — a well of animist knowing that survives in every terreiro, every kitchen garden, every story told after the children are supposed to be asleep.

When you read the Sítio do Picapau Amarelo now — if you ever do — listen for Nastácia. Not the Nastácia on the page, the frightened cook. The Nastácia underneath: the one who prays, who plants, who sews spirits into cloth, who keeps the door between worlds cracked open for children who don't yet know they're gifted.

She was always there. She was always the author. The books are just catching up.

illustrations.scene: Congada ceremony — Afro-Brazilian cultural sovereignty

items.name: The Compost and the Garden

sections.About:
Lobato's books are the compost. This grammar is the garden.

Composting does not mean destruction. It means transformation. You take organic matter — dead leaves, food scraps, things that have served their purpose — and you let them decompose. The heat of decomposition kills pathogens. What remains is rich, dark soil. You can grow anything in it.

Lobato's books contain brilliance and violence. Imagination and racism. Love for children and contempt for Blackness. These contradictions don't cancel each other out — they decompose together, producing something neither element could produce alone.

What grows from this compost is Nastácia's voice. Not the voice Lobato gave her (fearful, servile, comic) but the voice she always had (wise, faithful, strategic, loving, fierce). The garden doesn't look like the compost. It doesn't smell like it. But it could not exist without it.

This is the model for all composting grammars in this library: take the colonial text, let it decompose, and grow something new in the soil. The new thing remembers where it came from. It carries the nutrients of the original. But it belongs to the people who were composted by the original — and now, finally, they are doing the composting.

Nanã's principle: everything returns to the mud. Everything can be remade. The question is not whether the old stories will decompose — they will, they always do — but what we choose to plant in their place.
sections.For Readers:
You've now read thirty stories. Thirty moments from a life that was never written — until now.

This grammar takes Lobato's books and turns them inside out. Not to destroy them. Not to pretend they don't exist. To compost them: to let the beautiful and the violent decompose together until new soil forms, and to plant something in that soil that belongs to the woman who was always at the center of the story but was never allowed to speak.

The composting metaphor is not abstract. It is what Nanã — the oldest orixá, the grandmother of mud — teaches: everything that dies feeds what lives. Everything that falls apart makes room for what comes together. The old stories are falling apart. Not because we're attacking them — because they were already decomposing. Colonial stories always do, eventually. The question is: what grows next?

This grows. Nastácia's voice. Nastácia's faith. Nastácia's love for children who don't know they're gifted. Nastácia's herbs, and prayers, and letters to a mother she left in Bahia, and a river she talks to at night when no one's watching.

The cook was always the author.
The gesture was always the prayer.
The compost was always the garden.

Axé.

illustrations.scene: Market at Miners Beach — transformation and growth
